docs.title: Advanced Electricity & Magnetism Learning Lab
docs.text:
Welcome to the User Guide for the Advanced Electricity & Magnetism Learning Lab, a companion virtual laboratory covering Electrostatics, Magnetostatics, Electrodynamics, Electromagnetic Waves & their propagation in different media, and the electric & magnetic properties of materials, at the undergraduate and postgraduate level.

Try it live

Every experiment and workshop below has a matching interactive panel in the simulator: 🔬 Launch the Simulator

This guide provides, for each of the 18 experiments: the theoretical background, governing formula, apparatus schematic, a step-by-step procedure, a blank observation table and graph grid for your own readings, a maximum permissible error calculator, precautions, and viva-voce questions — followed by 7 hands-on industrial workshops that let you apply the same physics to realistic engineering scenarios, each scored against an optimal target with a printable completion certificate above 90%.

docs.title: Topics Covered
docs.text: Module Experiments Electrostatics Coulomb's Law · Electric Dipole Field & Potential · Gauss's Law · Parallel-Plate Capacitor & Dielectrics · Conductors & the Van de Graaff Generator Magnetostatics Biot–Savart Law · Circular Coil & Helmholtz Coils · Ampere's Law (Solenoid & Toroid) · Force Between Parallel Conductors Electrodynamics Faraday's Law · Motional EMF & Lenz's Law · RC/RL Transients · LCR Series Resonance EM Waves & Propagation Maxwell's Equations & Plane Waves · Propagation in Dielectric/Conducting Media (Skin Depth) · Reflection, Refraction & Brewster's Angle Electric & Magnetic Materials Dielectric Polarization (Clausius–Mossotti) · Magnetic Hysteresis & the B–H Curve

docs.text:
1. EMI/EMC Compliance Testing
  2. Transformer Design & Testing

docs.title: How to Use This Guide
docs.text:
Each experiment page follows the same structure: Aim → Theoretical Background → Governing Formula → Interactive Controls → Procedure → Observation Table → Graph → Calculations → Result → Precautions → Maximum Permissible Error → Viva-Voce Questions. Work through the simulator panel alongside the page, fill in your own observation tables and graphs (the graph grids accept a pasted photo of any physical meter/CRO trace for tracing, exactly as in the Advanced Laser & Optics Learning Lab).

See also the Formula Reference, Glossary, and Safety pages.

Prepared & maintained by Dr. S. K. Jain, Associate Professor in Physics, Department of Applied Sciences and Humanities, Invertis University, Bareilly, India, for the Academic Session 2026–27.

docs.title: Formula Reference
docs.text: A quick-reference sheet of the governing formula for every experiment in this lab, grouped by module.

docs.title: Electrostatics
docs.text: Experiment Formula Coulomb's Law \(F = q_1q_2/(4\pi\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r r^2)\) Electric Dipole \(V=p\cos\theta/(4\pi\varepsilon_0 r^2)\),   \(E_{axial}=2kp/r^3\),   \(E_{equatorial}=kp/r^3\) Gauss's Law \(\Phi_E = \oint \vec E\cdot d\vec A = Q_{enc}/\varepsilon_0\) Parallel-Plate Capacitor \(C = \varepsilon_r\varepsilon_0 A/d\) Van de Graaff / Conductors \(V_{dome}=Q/(4\pi\varepsilon_0 R)\),   \(E_{surface}=\sigma/\varepsilon_0\)

docs.title: Magnetostatics
docs.text: Experiment Formula Biot–Savart (finite wire) \(B=(\mu_0 I/4\pi a)(\sin\alpha_1+\sin\alpha_2)\) Circular Coil / Helmholtz \(B(x)=\mu_0NIR^2/[2(R^2+x^2)^{3/2}]\) Ampere's Law (Solenoid) \(B=\mu_0 nI\) Ampere's Law (Toroid) \(B(r)=\mu_0NI/(2\pi r)\) Force Between Parallel Wires \(F/L=\mu_0I_1I_2/(2\pi d)\)

docs.title: Electrodynamics
docs.text: Experiment Formula Faraday's Law \(\varepsilon=-N\,d\Phi_B/dt\) Motional EMF \(\varepsilon=BLv\),   \(F_{retard}=B^2L^2v/R\) RC Transient \(V_C(t)=\varepsilon_0(1-e^{-t/\tau})\),   \(\tau=RC\) RL Transient \(I(t)=(\varepsilon_0/R)(1-e^{-t/\tau})\),   \(\tau=L/R\) LCR Resonance \(\omega_0=1/\sqrt{LC}\),   \(Q=\omega_0L/R\)

docs.title: EM Waves & Propagation
docs.text: Experiment Formula Maxwell / Plane Waves \(c=1/\sqrt{\mu_0\varepsilon_0}\),   \(E_0/B_0=c\) Skin Depth \(\delta=\sqrt{2/(\omega\mu\sigma)}\) Brewster's Angle \(n_1\sin\theta_i=n_2\sin\theta_t\),   \(\theta_B=\arctan(n_2/n_1)\)

docs.title: Electric & Magnetic Materials
docs.text: Experiment Formula Dielectric Polarization \(P=\varepsilon_0(\varepsilon_r-1)E\),   \((\varepsilon_r-1)/(\varepsilon_r+2)=N\alpha/3\varepsilon_0\) Magnetic Hysteresis \(H=nI\); hysteresis loss/cycle/volume \(=\oint H\,dB\) (loop area)

docs.title: Universal Constants
docs.text:
Symbol Quantity Value \(\varepsilon_0\) Permittivity of free space \(8.854\times10^{-12}\) F/m \(\mu_0\) Permeability of free space \(4\pi\times10^{-7}\) T·m/A \(c\) Speed of light in vacuum \(2.998\times10^{8}\) m/s \(k = 1/4\pi\varepsilon_0\) Coulomb's constant \(8.988\times10^{9}\) N·m²/C²

docs.title: Glossary
docs.text: ← Back to BHP301 Course Page · Launch Simulator

docs.title: Glossary of Key Terms
docs.text: Definitions of the recurring physical concepts used across the 18 experiments and 7 workshops in this guide, in alphabetical order. Each entry links back to the experiment(s) where the term is most central.

docs.title: A
docs.text:
Ampere's circuital law — \(\oint \vec B\cdot d\vec l = \mu_0 I_{enc}\); used to find the field of highly symmetric current distributions. See Solenoid & Toroid.

Atomic/molecular polarizability (α) — The induced dipole moment per unit local electric field experienced by a single atom or molecule. See Dielectric Polarization.

docs.title: B
docs.text:
Biot–Savart law — Gives the magnetic field contribution from an infinitesimal current element; the magnetostatic analogue of Coulomb's law. See Biot–Savart.

Bound charge — Charge that appears at the surface (or within the volume) of a polarized dielectric due to the alignment of its internal dipoles, as distinct from free charge. See Dielectric Polarization.

Brewster's angle (θ_B) — The angle of incidence at which p-polarized light is not reflected at all at a dielectric interface. See Reflection, Refraction & Brewster's Angle.

docs.title: C
docs.text:
Clausius–Mossotti relation — Connects the macroscopic dielectric constant to the microscopic atomic polarizability, including the local-field correction. See Dielectric Polarization.

Coercivity (H_c) — The reverse magnetizing field required to bring the flux density of a magnetized material back to zero. See Magnetic Hysteresis.

Coulomb's law — The force between two point charges is proportional to the product of the charges and inversely proportional to the square of their separation. See Coulomb's Law.

docs.title: D
docs.text:
Dielectric constant (ε_r) — The relative permittivity of a material; the factor by which it increases a capacitor's capacitance relative to vacuum. See Parallel-Plate Capacitor.

Dipole moment (p) — A vector p = q·(2a) characterising the strength and orientation of an electric dipole. See Electric Dipole.

docs.title: E
docs.text: Eddy current — A circulating induced current set up in a bulk conductor by a changing magnetic flux, used constructively in eddy-current testing and braking, and minimised in transformer cores. See WS4: Eddy-Current NDT.

docs.title: F
docs.text: Faraday's law — The EMF induced in a circuit equals the negative rate of change of magnetic flux linkage through it. See Faraday's Law.

docs.title: G
docs.text: Gauss's law — The net electric flux through any closed surface equals the enclosed charge divided by ε₀. See Gauss's Law.

docs.title: H
docs.text:
Helmholtz coils — A pair of identical coaxial coils separated by a distance equal to their radius, producing a highly uniform field at their common centre. See Circular Coil & Helmholtz Coils.

Hysteresis loop — The closed B–H curve traced by a ferromagnetic material as the applied field is cycled; its enclosed area equals the energy dissipated per cycle. See Magnetic Hysteresis.

docs.title: L
docs.text: Lenz's law — The induced current always flows in the direction that opposes the change in flux producing it. See Faraday's Law and Motional EMF.

docs.title: M
docs.text:
Maxwell's equations — The four coupled equations (Gauss's law for E, Gauss's law for B, Faraday's law, Ampere–Maxwell law) that fully describe classical electromagnetism and predict electromagnetic waves. See Maxwell's Equations & Plane Waves.

Motional EMF — The EMF generated in a conductor moving through a magnetic field, ε = BLv for a rod on rails. See Motional EMF.

docs.title: Q
docs.text: Quality factor (Q) — A dimensionless measure of the sharpness of an LCR circuit's resonance, Q = ω₀L/R. See LCR Resonance.

docs.title: R
docs.text: Retentivity / Remanence (B_r) — The flux density remaining in a ferromagnetic material after the applied field is removed. See Magnetic Hysteresis.

docs.title: S
docs.text: Skin depth (δ) — The depth in a conductor at which an EM wave's amplitude falls to 1/e of its surface value. See Skin Depth.

docs.title: T
docs.text: Time constant (τ) — The characteristic time scale of an RC or RL transient (τ = RC or τ = L/R) at which the exponential response reaches (1 − 1/e) of its final change. See RC/RL Transients.

docs.title: V
docs.text:
VSWR (Voltage Standing-Wave Ratio) — A measure of impedance mismatch on a transmission line; VSWR = 1 for a perfectly matched load. See WS7: RF Impedance Matching.

docs.title: Safety
docs.text: ← Back to BHP301 Course Page · Launch Simulator

docs.title: Electrical & Magnetic Safety, Precautions & Potential Harm
docs.text:
Essential Reading Before Any Real Electrical Experiment

Several experiments and workshops in this lab correspond to real hazards when performed with physical apparatus — high voltages (Van de Graaff generator, HV breakdown testing), stored energy in capacitors and inductors, strong magnetic fields, and RF power. Read this section before touching any real apparatus.

Warning

Never touch the terminals of a charged capacitor, energised HV supply, or an inductor immediately after switch-off — stored energy can deliver a dangerous shock even after the source is disconnected.

Try it live

Explore the physics on the interactive simulator with real-time sliders and graphs: 🔬 Launch in Simulator

docs.title: Hazard Categories
docs.text: Hazard Typical source in this lab Risk Precaution Electric shock Charged capacitors, Van de Graaff dome, HV breakdown testing Cardiac/muscular injury, burns De-energize and discharge through a bleeder resistor before touching; never work alone on real HV apparatus Capacitor/inductor stored energy RC/RL transient circuits, LCR resonance Shock or component explosion if shorted carelessly Always discharge capacitors through a resistor, never a direct short; respect voltage/current ratings Strong magnetic fields Solenoids, Helmholtz coils, electromagnets Interference with pacemakers/implants; ferromagnetic tool/object projectiles Keep persons with medical implants away from strong-field areas; secure loose ferromagnetic objects High-frequency RF Antenna workshop, RF impedance-matching workshop RF burns, tissue heating at high power Never approach an energized transmitting antenna; follow RF-exposure guidelines Corona/ozone Van de Graaff generator, sharp-point conductors at high voltage Ozone irritation, unwanted discharge/fire risk Operate in ventilated spaces; keep flammable materials away from active corona regions Eye/skin exposure High-voltage arcs, breakdown testing UV flash burns, arc-flash injury Use appropriate face/eye protection during real breakdown testing; maintain safe standoff distance

docs.title: General Precautions for Real Apparatus
docs.text:
• Always treat a capacitor or inductor as charged/energized until you have personally verified otherwise with a meter, and discharge it safely through an appropriate resistor before handling.
  • Never defeat interlocks or safety enclosures on high-voltage equipment, even "just to look."
  • Keep one hand in your pocket when probing live circuits, to reduce the risk of a shock path across the chest.
  • Ensure a competent second person is present whenever working with voltages above extra-low-voltage limits.
  • Ground/earth exposed conductive enclosures of test equipment before energizing.
  • Know the location of the nearest emergency power cut-off before starting any high-power experiment.
  • For any workshop scenario in this guide involving genuinely hazardous voltages, currents, or RF power (HV insulation testing, transformer testing, antenna transmission, EMI/EMC testing), treat the numbers simulated here as illustrative only — real work of this kind requires certified equipment, training, and supervision, and is not something to attempt from this guide alone.

docs.title: Reporting
docs.text:
If you are ever unsure whether a piece of real apparatus is safe to touch or energize, stop and ask your laboratory supervisor before proceeding. It is always acceptable to pause an experiment for a safety check.

docs.title: Ampere's Circuital Law — Solenoid & Toroid
docs.text: ← Back to BHP301 Course Page · Launch Simulator · 📝 Open Lab Record & Viva-Voce Booklet (PDF) (print just this experiment's pages when you sit down at the simulator)

docs.title: Ampere's Circuital Law — Field Inside a Solenoid & a Toroid
docs.text:
SOURCE / CONTROL APPARATUS (virtual setup) PROBE / DETECTOR A virtual long solenoid (adjustable length, turns density, current) with an interior/exterior field probe, and a toroid with adjustable major/minor radius and turns, both instrumented with Amperian-loop visualisation.

Apparatus schematic · A virtual long solenoid (adjustable length, turns density, current) with an interior/exterior field probe, and a toroid with adjustable major/minor radius and turns, both instrumented with Amperian-loop visualisation.

docs.title: Aim
docs.text: To verify Ampere's circuital law by computing the magnetic field inside a long solenoid and a toroid, and to study the field's uniformity and its dependence on turns density and current.

docs.title: Theoretical Background
docs.text: Ampere's circuital law states ∮ B·dl = μ₀ I_enc around any closed Amperian loop, where I_enc is the current enclosed. For a long, tightly-wound solenoid with n turns per unit length, a rectangular Amperian loop (one side inside, one outside, both parallel to the axis) gives a uniform interior field B = μ₀nI, with negligible field outside the winding — an excellent approximation for a solenoid whose length far exceeds its radius. A toroid (a solenoid bent into a closed ring) confines the field entirely within the torus's core with zero field elsewhere; applying Ampere's law to a circular loop of radius r within the core gives B(r) = μ₀NI/2πr, which is not perfectly uniform across the core's cross-section (it is stronger on the inner side) unless the core radius is much larger than the tube radius.

docs.title: Governing Formula
docs.text: \[ Solenoid: B = μ₀ n I    |    Toroid: B(r) = μ₀ N I / (2πr) \] Symbol Meaning B magnetic field (T) n turns per unit length of the solenoid (turns/m) N total turns of the toroid I current (A) r radial distance from the toroid's axis, within the core (m)

docs.title: Interactive Controls
docs.text:
Launch the simulator and use the following controls on the Ampere's Circuital Law — Field Inside a Solenoid & a Toroid panel:

Control Symbol Range Default Solenoid turns density n 5–100 turns/cm 30 turns/cm Current I 0.1–5 A 1 A Probe position (0=centre, 1=at the end, 2=outside) x 0–2 0 Toroid major radius R 5–30 cm 15 cm Toroid total turns N_t 50–1000 300

docs.title: Procedure
docs.text:
1. For the solenoid, place the probe well inside (away from the ends) and vary n and I independently, verifying B = μ₀nI in each case.
  2. Move the probe toward one end of the solenoid and note the field dropping to roughly half its central value at the very end (edge effect), then to nearly zero just outside.
  3. For the toroid, place the probe inside the core and vary the radial position r; verify B ∝ 1/r within the core cross-section.
  4. Confirm the toroid's field is exactly zero for a probe placed outside the core (either inside the central hole or outside the outer radius).
  5. Compare the interior solenoid field at the same nI product with different individual choices of n and I to check only the product matters.
  6. Sketch a suitable rectangular Amperian loop for the solenoid and a circular one for the toroid, and verify ∮B·dl = μ₀I_enc numerically in both cases.

docs.title: Graph
docs.text:
Solenoid Field Along the Axis (end effects) — flat top, drops to ~0.5 at each end, ~0 outside

📋 Click the grid, then paste (Ctrl+V) a copied photo of your reading/meter trace to overlay it for tracing — nothing is uploaded, it stays on your screen only. Overlay opacity Clear pasted image Solenoid Field Along the Axis (end effects) Axial position (fraction of length) → B / B_centre → 15×15 grid — choose your own scale per square; plot from your observation table. Expected trend: flat top, drops to ~0.5 at each end, ~0 outside

docs.title: Calculations
docs.text: Use the governing formula above with your tabulated observations to compute the required result for each row. Show at least one complete sample calculation with units.

docs.title: Result
docs.text: | Configuration | Field uniformity | Verified law | Remarks | |---|---|---|---|| | | | | |

docs.title: Precautions
docs.text:
• Ensure the solenoid is 'long' (length ≫ radius) before applying the idealized formula B = μ₀nI; note deviations for short coils.
  • For the toroid, keep the probe strictly within the core cross-section; readings just outside the winding should read zero.
  • Keep turns tightly and uniformly wound in the model — gaps between turns would break the assumed symmetry.
  • Distinguish n (turns per unit length, used for the solenoid) from N (total turns, used for the toroid formula) carefully.

docs.title: Maximum Permissible Error
docs.text:
\[ ΔB/B = Δn/n + ΔI/I \]

B = μ₀nI is a simple product, so fractional errors in n and I add directly.

docs.title: Viva-Voce Questions
docs.text:
1. State Ampere's circuital law and explain the choice of Amperian loop used to derive the solenoid's field.
  2. Why is the field of an ideal solenoid exactly zero outside the winding, but not exactly zero for a real, finite solenoid?
  3. Derive the toroid's field formula using a circular Amperian loop.
  4. Why is a toroid's field not perfectly uniform across its cross-section, unlike an idealized long solenoid?
  5. Under what conditions does Ampere's law alone suffice to determine B, without needing the full Biot–Savart integral?
  6. How is Ampere's law modified for time-varying fields (Maxwell's displacement-current correction)?

Author & Maintainer: Dr. S. K. Jain, Associate Professor in Physics, Department of Applied Sciences and Humanities, Invertis University, Bareilly, India 📧 sanjeev.j@invertis.org

docs.title: Biot–Savart Law
docs.text: ← Back to BHP301 Course Page · Launch Simulator · 📝 Open Lab Record & Viva-Voce Booklet (PDF) (print just this experiment's pages when you sit down at the simulator)

docs.title: Biot–Savart Law — Magnetic Field Due to a Current-Carrying Wire
docs.text:
SOURCE / CONTROL APPARATUS (virtual setup) PROBE / DETECTOR A virtual straight current-carrying wire of adjustable length and current, with a field probe that can be placed at any perpendicular distance a; the simulator numerically integrates the Biot–Savart law along the wire's length.

Apparatus schematic · A virtual straight current-carrying wire of adjustable length and current, with a field probe that can be placed at any perpendicular distance a; the simulator numerically integrates the Biot–Savart law along the wire's length.

docs.title: Aim
docs.text: To compute the magnetic field due to a finite straight current-carrying wire using the Biot–Savart law, verify the perpendicular-distance dependence, and recover Ampere's infinite-wire result as a limiting case.

docs.title: Theoretical Background
docs.text: The Biot–Savart law gives the magnetic field contribution dB at a field point due to a current element I·dl as dB = (μ₀/4π)· I dl × ŕ / r², directed perpendicular to both the current element and the line joining it to the field point. Integrating along a finite straight wire of length L, the field at perpendicular distance a from the wire is B = (μ₀I/4πa)·(sinα₁ + sinα₂), where α₁ and α₂ are the angles subtended by the wire's two ends at the field point. As the wire's length is taken to infinity (α₁, α₂ → 90°) this reduces to the familiar infinite-wire result B = μ₀I/2πa, which can also be obtained far more simply from Ampere's circuital law when the geometry has enough symmetry.

docs.title: Governing Formula
docs.text: \[ B = (μ₀I / 4πa)(sinα₁ + sinα₂) \] Symbol Meaning B magnetic field at the probe point (T) I current in the wire (A) a perpendicular distance from the wire (m) α₁, α₂ angles subtended by the wire's two ends at the field point μ₀ permeability of free space (4π×10⁻⁷ T·m/A)

docs.title: Interactive Controls
docs.text:
Launch the simulator and use the following controls on the Biot–Savart Law — Magnetic Field Due to a Current-Carrying Wire panel:

Control Symbol Range Default Current I 0.5–10 A 2 A Perpendicular distance a 1–30 cm 10 cm Wire length L 10–200 cm 40 cm

docs.title: Procedure
docs.text:
1. Fix a short wire length L and current I, and vary the probe distance a; plot B vs 1/a and check linearity near the wire's midpoint.
  2. Fix a and I, and increase L progressively; observe B approaching the infinite-wire value μ₀I/2πa asymptotically.
  3. At fixed a and L, vary I and verify B ∝ I.
  4. Move the probe off the wire's perpendicular bisector (nearer one end) and observe the asymmetric α₁, α₂ and resulting change in B.
  5. Compare a numerically Biot–Savart-integrated value with the closed-form (sinα₁+sinα₂) formula for several geometries.
  6. Use the right-hand rule to verify the predicted direction of B at several probe positions around the wire.

docs.title: Graph
docs.text:
B vs 1/a at fixed I, L — approx. linear near mid-span

📋 Click the grid, then paste (Ctrl+V) a copied photo of your reading/meter trace to overlay it for tracing — nothing is uploaded, it stays on your screen only. Overlay opacity Clear pasted image B vs 1/a at fixed I, L 1/a (cm⁻¹) → B (×10⁻⁵ T) → 15×15 grid — choose your own scale per square; plot from your observation table. Expected trend: approx. linear near mid-span

docs.title: Calculations
docs.text: Use the governing formula above with your tabulated observations to compute the required result for each row. Show at least one complete sample calculation with units.

docs.title: Result
docs.text: | Wire length L | B (finite wire) | B (infinite-wire formula) | % difference | |---|---|---|---|| | | | | |

docs.title: Precautions
docs.text:
• Keep the field point on the wire's perpendicular bisector unless deliberately studying the asymmetric case.
  • Note the finite-wire formula converges to the infinite-wire result only when L ≫ a.
  • Apply the right-hand (or Maxwell's corkscrew) rule consistently to fix the field's direction, not just its magnitude.
  • Keep the current within a realistic laboratory range to keep the field small enough for safe virtual probes.

docs.title: Maximum Permissible Error
docs.text:
\[ ΔB/B = ΔI/I + Δa/a \]

For the infinite-wire limit B = μ₀I/2πa, a simple quotient, so fractional errors in I and a add.

docs.title: Viva-Voce Questions
docs.text:
1. State the Biot–Savart law in vector form and compare its structure with Coulomb's law.
  2. Derive the infinite-wire field formula B = μ₀I/2πa as a limiting case of the finite-wire result.
  3. In which direction does B point around a current-carrying wire, and which rule fixes this?
  4. Why is the field due to a straight wire independent of its cross-sectional shape (for a thin wire)?
  5. How would the Biot–Savart integral change for a wire bent into an L-shape?
  6. Compare and contrast the Biot–Savart law with Ampere's circuital law — when is each more convenient to use?

docs.title: Circular Coil & Helmholtz Coils
docs.text: ← Back to BHP301 Course Page · Launch Simulator · 📝 Open Lab Record & Viva-Voce Booklet (PDF) (print just this experiment's pages when you sit down at the simulator)

docs.title: Magnetic Field on the Axis of a Circular Coil & Helmholtz Coils
docs.text:
SOURCE / CONTROL APPARATUS (virtual setup) PROBE / DETECTOR A single virtual circular coil (adjustable R, N, I) with an axial field probe, and a switchable Helmholtz-pair configuration with adjustable coil separation, plotting B(x) along the common axis.

Apparatus schematic · A single virtual circular coil (adjustable R, N, I) with an axial field probe, and a switchable Helmholtz-pair configuration with adjustable coil separation, plotting B(x) along the common axis.

docs.title: Aim
docs.text: To study the magnetic field along the axis of a single circular current loop, locate the point of maximum field, and demonstrate how a Helmholtz pair produces a highly uniform field at its centre.

docs.title: Theoretical Background
docs.text: The magnetic field on the axis of a circular coil of radius R and N turns carrying current I, at an axial distance x from its centre, is B(x) = μ₀NIR² / [2(R²+x²)^(3/2)]. At the centre (x = 0) this reduces to B₀ = μ₀NI/2R, the maximum value. Two identical coils placed coaxially and separated by a distance equal to their common radius R (the 'Helmholtz' condition) produce a combined field whose second derivative with respect to x vanishes at the midpoint — making the field remarkably uniform over a useful volume between the coils, which is why Helmholtz coils are the standard tool for producing a known, controllable, uniform field for calibrating magnetometers and cancelling the Earth's field in sensitive experiments.

docs.title: Governing Formula
docs.text: \[ B(x) = μ₀ N I R² / [2(R² + x²)^{3/2}] \] Symbol Meaning B(x) axial field at distance x from the coil centre (T) N number of turns I current (A) R coil radius (m) x axial distance from the coil's centre (m)

docs.title: Interactive Controls
docs.text:
Launch the simulator and use the following controls on the Magnetic Field on the Axis of a Circular Coil & Helmholtz Coils panel:

Control Symbol Range Default Coil radius R 5–30 cm 10 cm Number of turns N 1–500 100 Current I 0.1–5 A 1 A Axial distance from centre x -30–30 cm 0 cm Helmholtz coil separation (mode 2) d 5–30 cm 10 cm

docs.title: Procedure
docs.text:
1. For a single coil, sweep x from −30 cm to +30 cm and plot B(x); verify the peak occurs at x = 0 with value μ₀NI/2R.
  2. Verify B(0) ∝ N and B(0) ∝ I by varying each independently.
  3. Switch to the Helmholtz configuration with separation d = R and plot the combined field along the axis; note the flat region near the midpoint.
  4. Change the separation away from d = R (e.g. d = 0.5R or 2R) and observe the field profile becoming non-uniform (dip or double-hump).
  5. Estimate the useful 'uniform-field volume' (region where B stays within 1% of the central value) for the ideal Helmholtz separation.
  6. Compute the theoretical central field of the Helmholtz pair and compare with the simulator's numerically-integrated value.

docs.title: Result

docs.title: Precautions
docs.text:
• Keep R, N and I fixed while sweeping x to isolate the axial-distance dependence cleanly.
  • For the Helmholtz study, change only the coil separation d between runs, not R, N or I.
  • Note the field falls off increasingly steeply for |x| ≫ R; keep this in mind when defining the 'uniform region'.
  • Remember the Helmholtz condition (d = R) is optimal only for the specific criterion of a vanishing second derivative at the centre — other criteria give slightly different optimal spacings.

docs.text:
\[ ΔB₀/B₀ = ΔN/N + ΔI/I + ΔR/R \]

B₀ = μ₀NI/2R at the coil centre is a simple product/quotient, so fractional errors add.

docs.title: Viva-Voce Questions
docs.text:
1. Derive the on-axis field of a circular current loop starting from the Biot–Savart law.
  2. At what axial distance does B(x) fall to half its central value, for a single coil?
  3. Why is the Helmholtz coil separation chosen equal to the coil radius?
  4. What physical quantity being zero at the midpoint makes the Helmholtz field so uniform there?
  5. How would you use a Helmholtz pair to cancel the Earth's magnetic field in a sensitive experiment?
  6. Compare the on-axis field of a coil with that of a long solenoid — under what condition does one reduce to the other?

docs.title: Charge Distribution on Conductors & the Van de Graaff Generator
docs.text: ← Back to BHP301 Course Page · Launch Simulator · 📝 Open Lab Record & Viva-Voce Booklet (PDF) (print just this experiment's pages when you sit down at the simulator)

docs.title: Charge Distribution on Conductors & the Van de Graaff Generator
docs.text:
SOURCE / CONTROL APPARATUS (virtual setup) PROBE / DETECTOR A set of virtual conductors (sphere, ellipsoid, conductor-with- a-point) whose surface charge density can be visualised as a heat-map, plus a Van de Graaff generator model with an adjustable belt-charging rate and dome radius.

Apparatus schematic · A set of virtual conductors (sphere, ellipsoid, conductor-with-a-point) whose surface charge density can be visualised as a heat-map, plus a Van de Graaff generator model with an adjustable belt-charging rate and dome radius.

docs.title: Aim
docs.text: To study how charge distributes itself over the surface of conductors of different shapes at electrostatic equilibrium, and to understand the principle of the Van de Graaff generator for building up very high potentials.

docs.title: Theoretical Background
docs.text: At electrostatic equilibrium, the electric field inside a conductor is zero and any excess charge resides entirely on its outer surface, arranging itself so the surface is an equipotential. Surface charge density σ is not uniform for a non-spherical conductor — it is highest where the surface curvature is greatest (sharp points and edges), which is why the field just outside a sharp point (E = σ/ε₀) can become large enough to ionize the surrounding air ('action at points', used in lightning rods and van de Graaff brush discharge). A Van de Graaff generator exploits the fact that if a charged conductor is brought inside a larger hollow conductor and touched to its inner surface, all its charge transfers to the outer surface of the larger conductor, however much charge the outer shell already carries — the potential can therefore be raised indefinitely by repeatedly carrying small charges up an insulating belt into the hollow dome.

docs.title: Governing Formula
docs.text: \[ V_dome = Q / (4πε₀ R),   E_surface = σ/ε₀ \] Symbol Meaning V_dome potential of the charged spherical dome (V) Q total charge delivered to the dome (C) R radius of the dome (m) σ local surface charge density (C/m²)

docs.title: Interactive Controls
docs.text:
Launch the simulator and use the following controls on the Charge Distribution on Conductors & the Van de Graaff Generator panel:

Control Symbol Range Default Dome radius R 10–50 cm 25 cm Belt charging rate rate 1–20 nC/s 5 nC/s Charging time t 0–60 s 20 s Test conductor tip radius (for point-discharge study) r_tip 0.5–20 mm 2 mm

docs.title: Procedure
docs.text:
1. Charge the Van de Graaff dome for increasing durations and plot dome potential V versus charging time t; verify V ∝ t (constant charging rate).
  2. For a fixed total charge, vary the dome radius R and verify V ∝ 1/R.
  3. Observe the surface-charge heat-map on an ellipsoidal conductor and note that charge density is highest at the sharper end.
  4. Reduce the tip radius of a test conductor and note the field at the tip rising as 1/r_tip until corona discharge (visualised as a glow) sets in.
  5. Verify numerically that the field just inside the conductor's surface is zero at every point, regardless of shape.
  6. Bring a small pre-charged test sphere inside the dome and touch it to the inner wall; confirm all of its charge transfers to the dome's outer surface.

docs.text: | S. No. | Charging time t (s) | Rate (nC/s) | Total charge Q (nC) | Dome radius R (cm) | Dome potential V (kV) | |---|---|---|---|---|---|| | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | |

docs.text: Use the governing formula above with your tabulated observations to compute the required result for each row. Show at least one complete sample calculation with units.

docs.text: | Quantity studied | Observed trend | Theoretical prediction | Agreement | |---|---|---|---|| | | | | |

docs.title: Precautions
docs.text:
• Keep the charging rate constant while studying the V–t relationship; vary only one control at a time.
  • Note that real Van de Graaff generators are limited by the dielectric breakdown of surrounding air, not by the belt mechanism itself.
  • Remember the field inside any conductor is exactly zero at equilibrium — a common source of confusion is expecting a small residual field.
  • When studying the sharp-point tip, understand this is an idealization; real points are never perfectly sharp.

docs.title: Maximum Permissible Error
docs.text:
\[ ΔV/V = Δrate/rate + Δt/t + ΔR/R \]

V = kQ/R with Q = rate×t (charging rate times time), so fractional errors in rate, time and R all add.

docs.title: Viva-Voce Questions
docs.text:
1. Why must the electric field inside a conductor in electrostatic equilibrium be zero?
  2. Explain why charge density is greatest at the sharpest points of a charged conductor.
  3. How does a Van de Graaff generator manage to raise the dome to a very high potential regardless of the charge already present on it?
  4. What is corona discharge and why does it limit the maximum achievable potential?
  5. State the boundary condition on E just outside a charged conductor's surface in terms of σ.
  6. Why is the interior of a hollow charged conductor field-free even if the shell is not spherical (Faraday cage)?

docs.title: Coulomb's Law & Force Between Point Charges
docs.text: ← Back to BHP301 Course Page · Launch Simulator · 📝 Open Lab Record & Viva-Voce Booklet (PDF) (print just this experiment's pages when you sit down at the simulator)

docs.title: Coulomb's Law & Force Between Point Charges
docs.text:
SOURCE / CONTROL APPARATUS (virtual setup) PROBE / DETECTOR A torsion-balance (Coulomb balance) analogue: a charged pith ball on a torsion fibre is deflected by a second charged ball brought to a variable separation r on an insulating rail; the fibre's restoring torque at equilibrium is proportional to F.

Apparatus schematic · A torsion-balance (Coulomb balance) analogue: a charged pith ball on a torsion fibre is deflected by a second charged ball brought to a variable separation r on an insulating rail; the fibre's restoring torque at equilibrium is proportional to F.

docs.title: Aim
docs.text: To verify the inverse-square dependence of the electrostatic force between two point charges on their separation, and its direct proportionality to the product of the charges, using a torsion-balance style virtual setup.

docs.title: Theoretical Background
docs.text: Coulomb's law states that the force between two point charges q1 and q2 separated by a distance r in a medium of permittivity ε acts along the line joining them, is directly proportional to the product of the charges and inversely proportional to the square of the distance between them: F = (1/4πε₀)·q1q2/r² in free space (or F = (1/4πε₀εr)·q1q2/r² in a dielectric medium of relative permittivity εr). The force is repulsive for like charges and attractive for unlike charges. This law is the electrostatic foundation from which Gauss's law, the electric field, and the potential of a point charge are all derived, and it underlies the entire multipole expansion used later for dipoles and extended charge distributions.

docs.title: Governing Formula
docs.text: \[ F = q₁q₂ / (4πε₀εr r²) \] Symbol Meaning F electrostatic force between the charges (N) q₁, q₂ the two point charges (C) r separation between the charges (m) ε₀ permittivity of free space (8.854×10⁻¹² F/m) εr relative permittivity of the surrounding medium

docs.title: Interactive Controls
docs.text:
Launch the simulator and use the following controls on the Coulomb's Law & Force Between Point Charges panel:

Control Symbol Range Default Charge 1 q₁ 1–50 nC 10 nC Charge 2 q₂ 1–50 nC 10 nC Separation r 2–30 cm 10 cm Medium relative permittivity εr 1–80 1

docs.title: Procedure
docs.text:
1. Set the medium to vacuum/air (εr = 1) and fix q₁ = q₂ = 10 nC.
  2. Vary the separation r from 2 cm to 30 cm in steps of 2 cm, noting the computed force F at each step.
  3. Plot log F versus log r and determine the slope; it should equal −2, confirming the inverse-square law.
  4. Fix r = 10 cm and vary q₂ from 5 nC to 50 nC, keeping q₁ fixed, and verify F ∝ q₂ (a straight line through the origin on an F vs q₂ plot).
  5. Change the medium permittivity εr (e.g. to 80 for water) and observe the reduction in force at fixed q and r.
  6. Reverse the sign of one charge and note that the force arrow reverses from repulsive to attractive.

docs.title: Graph
docs.text:
log F vs log r (slope method) — slope ≈ -2

📋 Click the grid, then paste (Ctrl+V) a copied photo of your reading/meter trace to overlay it for tracing — nothing is uploaded, it stays on your screen only. Overlay opacity Clear pasted image log F vs log r (slope method) log₁₀ r (r in m) → log₁₀ F (F in N) → 15×15 grid — choose your own scale per square; plot from your observation table. Expected trend: slope ≈ -2

docs.text: Use the governing formula above with your tabulated observations to compute the required result for each row. Show at least one complete sample calculation with units.

docs.title: Result
docs.text: | Quantity | Slope of log F vs log r | Expected value | % deviation | |---|---|---|---|| | | | | |

docs.title: Precautions
docs.text:
• Keep the charges' magnitudes fixed while varying only one parameter at a time.
  • Ensure the separation r is always much larger than the physical size of the charged spheres so the point-charge approximation holds.
  • Note the sign convention: like charges repel (positive F, arrow pointing outward) and unlike charges attract.
  • Discharge and re-zero the torsion fibre between widely different charge settings to avoid residual twist error.

docs.title: Maximum Permissible Error
docs.text:
\[ ΔF/F = Δq₁/q₁ + Δq₂/q₂ + 2Δr/r \]

F ∝ q₁q₂/r² is a product/quotient of measured quantities, with r entering squared, so its fractional error carries a factor of 2.

docs.title: Viva-Voce Questions
docs.text:
1. State Coulomb's law in vector form and explain each term.
  2. Why does the electrostatic force obey an inverse-square law rather than, say, an inverse-cube law?
  3. How does the force change if the experiment is repeated in a dielectric medium instead of vacuum?
  4. Derive the electric field of a point charge starting from Coulomb's law.
  5. Compare the magnitude of the electrostatic and gravitational forces between two electrons.
  6. What is the principle of superposition, and how does it apply to a system of more than two charges?

docs.title: Dielectric Polarization & Clausius–Mossotti Relation
docs.text: ← Back to BHP301 Course Page · Launch Simulator · 📝 Open Lab Record & Viva-Voce Booklet (PDF) (print just this experiment's pages when you sit down at the simulator)

docs.title: Dielectric Polarization & the Clausius–Mossotti Relation
docs.text:
SOURCE / CONTROL APPARATUS (virtual setup) PROBE / DETECTOR A virtual slab of dielectric material placed between capacitor plates with a molecular-scale zoom showing individual dipoles aligning under the applied field, plus a control panel for number density N and atomic polarizability α feeding the Clausius–Mossotti calculator.

Apparatus schematic · A virtual slab of dielectric material placed between capacitor plates with a molecular-scale zoom showing individual dipoles aligning under the applied field, plus a control panel for number density N and atomic polarizability α feeding the Clausius–Mossotti calculator.

docs.title: Aim
docs.text: To study how an external electric field polarizes a dielectric material, relate the macroscopic polarization to the microscopic atomic polarizability using the Clausius–Mossotti relation, and observe the resulting bound charges.

docs.title: Theoretical Background
docs.text: When a dielectric is placed in an external field E, its constituent atoms/molecules develop induced dipole moments (or, for polar molecules, their permanent dipoles partially align), producing a macroscopic polarization P = ε₀χe·E, where χe = εr − 1 is the electric susceptibility. This polarization manifests as bound surface charge σ_b = P·n̂ at the dielectric's surfaces (and bound volume charge where P is non-uniform), which partially cancels the external field inside the material — the origin of the dielectric constant's field-reducing effect seen in the capacitor experiment. The microscopic atomic polarizability α (dipole moment induced per unit local field) is related to the macroscopic εr through the Clausius–Mossotti relation: (εr−1)/(εr+2) = Nα/3ε₀, where N is the number density of polarizable atoms/molecules — this correctly accounts for the fact that each molecule sees not just the applied field but also the field of all the other induced dipoles around it (the local-field correction).

docs.title: Governing Formula
docs.text: \[ P = ε₀(εr−1)E,     (εr−1)/(εr+2) = Nα/(3ε₀) \] Symbol Meaning P macroscopic polarization (C/m²) εr relative permittivity (dielectric constant) E applied macroscopic field (V/m) N number density of molecules (m⁻³) α molecular polarizability (C·m²/V)

docs.title: Interactive Controls
docs.text:
Launch the simulator and use the following controls on the Dielectric Polarization & the Clausius–Mossotti Relation panel:

Control Symbol Range Default Number density N 1–50 ×10²⁸ m⁻³ 20 ×10²⁸ m⁻³ Molecular polarizability α 1–50 ×10⁻⁴⁰ C·m²/V 15 ×10⁻⁴⁰ C·m²/V Applied field E 1–100 kV/m 20 kV/m

docs.title: Procedure
docs.text:
1. For a chosen N and α, use the Clausius–Mossotti relation to compute εr, and verify it against the simulator's directly-modelled dielectric response.
  2. Increase N (denser material) and observe εr rising, eventually approaching the 'polarization catastrophe' limit where Nα/3ε₀ → 1.
  3. At fixed N, α, apply an external field E and read off the induced polarization P; verify P = ε₀(εr−1)E.
  4. Zoom into the molecular view and observe individual dipoles growing (or aligning, for polar molecules) proportionally with E.
  5. Compute the bound surface charge density σ_b = P at the dielectric's outer faces and compare it with the field-reduction observed in the parallel-plate-capacitor experiment.
  6. Compare the Clausius–Mossotti prediction with the simpler (and less accurate at high Nα) approximation εr − 1 ≈ Nα/ε₀ that ignores the local-field correction.

docs.title: Observation Table
docs.text: | S. No. | N (×10²⁸ m⁻³) | α (×10⁻⁴⁰ C·m²/V) | εr (Clausius–Mossotti) | Applied field E (kV/m) | Polarization P (μC/m²) | |---|---|---|---|---|---|| | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | |

docs.title: Graph
docs.text:
Dielectric Constant vs Number Density N — εr rises steeply as Nα/3ε₀ → 1

Overlay opacity Clear pasted image Dielectric Constant vs Number Density N N (×10²⁸ m⁻³) → εr → 15×15 grid — choose your own scale per square; plot from your observation table. Expected trend: εr rises steeply as Nα/3ε₀ → 1

docs.title: Calculations
docs.text: Use the governing formula above with your tabulated observations to compute the required result for each row. Show at least one complete sample calculation with units.

docs.title: Result
docs.text: | Case | εr computed | Bound charge σ_b | Remarks | |---|---|---|---|| | | | | |

docs.title: Precautions
docs.text:
• Keep Nα/3ε₀ below 1 for a physically stable dielectric; values approaching or exceeding 1 signal the (unphysical, in this simple model) polarization catastrophe/ferroelectric-like instability.
  • Distinguish clearly between the macroscopic applied field E and the larger local field seen by each individual molecule.
  • Vary only one of N, α, E at a time when tabulating results.
  • Remember bound charges are real charges bound to atoms, distinct from free charges on the capacitor plates.

docs.title: Maximum Permissible Error
docs.text:
\[ via Clausius–Mossotti; Δεr estimated numerically from ΔN/N + Δα/α \]

εr is a nonlinear function of Nα, so its propagated error is evaluated numerically from the fractional errors of N and α rather than a simple closed form.

docs.title: Viva-Voce Questions
docs.text:
1. Define polarization P and relate it to the electric susceptibility χe.
  2. Derive the Clausius–Mossotti relation, explaining the role of the local (Lorentz) field correction.
  3. What is meant by 'bound charge', and where does it appear on/in a polarized dielectric?
  4. Why does the simple relation εr−1 ≈ Nα/ε₀ (without the local-field correction) become inaccurate at high densities?
  5. How does the Clausius–Mossotti relation connect to the Lorentz–Lorenz relation for the refractive index?
  6. Explain qualitatively what a 'polarization catastrophe' represents physically (link to ferroelectricity).

docs.title: Electric Field & Potential Due to an Electric Dipole
docs.text: ← Back to BHP301 Course Page · Launch Simulator · 📝 Open Lab Record & Viva-Voce Booklet (PDF) (print just this experiment's pages when you sit down at the simulator)

docs.title: Electric Field & Potential Due to an Electric Dipole
docs.text:
SOURCE / CONTROL APPARATUS (virtual setup) PROBE / DETECTOR A virtual field-mapping grid with two fixed opposite point charges (±q) at adjustable separation 2a; a probe point can be dragged to any (r, θ) to read the resultant field vector and potential, with field lines rendered live.

Apparatus schematic · A virtual field-mapping grid with two fixed opposite point charges (±q) at adjustable separation 2a; a probe point can be dragged to any (r, θ) to read the resultant field vector and potential, with field lines rendered live.

docs.title: Aim
docs.text: To compute and plot the electric field and potential of an electric dipole along its axial and equatorial lines, and to study the angular variation of the field at a fixed distance.

docs.title: Theoretical Background
docs.text: An electric dipole consists of two equal and opposite charges +q and −q separated by a small distance 2a, with dipole moment p = q·(2a) directed from −q to +q. For distances r ≫ a, the potential at a point making angle θ with the dipole axis is V = p·cosθ/(4πε₀r²), and the field has a radial component E_r = 2p·cosθ/(4πε₀r³) and a tangential component E_θ = p·sinθ/(4πε₀r³). On the axial line (θ = 0) the field is E_axial = 2kp/r³, twice the equatorial-line field E_equatorial = kp/r³ (θ = 90°), and the two fields are oppositely directed relative to p. This 1/r³ falloff (steeper than the 1/r² of a point charge) is a signature of the dipole and is the basis for understanding polar-molecule interactions and dielectric polarization.

docs.title: Governing Formula
docs.text: \[ V = p·cosθ / (4πε₀ r²),  E_axial = 2kp/r³,  E_equatorial = kp/r³ \] Symbol Meaning p dipole moment, p = q·2a (C·m) r distance from the dipole centre (m) θ angle from the dipole axis k 1/4πε₀ = 8.988×10⁹ N·m²/C²

docs.title: Interactive Controls
docs.text:
Launch the simulator and use the following controls on the Electric Field & Potential Due to an Electric Dipole panel:

Control Symbol Range Default Charge magnitude q 1–20 nC 5 nC Charge separation 2a 1–10 cm 4 cm Probe distance r 5–50 cm 20 cm Probe angle from axis θ 0–180 ° 0 °

docs.title: Procedure
docs.text:
1. Set the probe on the axial line (θ = 0°) and record E and V as r is increased from 5 cm to 50 cm.
  2. Plot log E versus log r for the axial case and verify the slope is −3 (not −2 as for a single point charge).
  3. Move the probe to the equatorial line (θ = 90°) and confirm V ≈ 0 there at every r.
  4. At a fixed r = 20 cm, sweep θ from 0° to 180° and tabulate E and V; note the sign reversal of V past θ = 90°.
  5. Compare the axial field magnitude with the equatorial field magnitude at the same r; verify the 2:1 ratio.
  6. Increase the separation 2a and confirm the point-dipole formulas remain accurate only while r ≫ a.

docs.text:
Axial Field E vs r (log-log) — slope ≈ -3

📋 Click the grid, then paste (Ctrl+V) a copied photo of your reading/meter trace to overlay it for tracing — nothing is uploaded, it stays on your screen only. Overlay opacity Clear pasted image Axial Field E vs r (log-log) log₁₀ r → log₁₀ E → 15×15 grid — choose your own scale per square; plot from your observation table. Expected trend: slope ≈ -3

docs.title: Calculations
docs.text: Use the governing formula above with your tabulated observations to compute the required result for each row. Show at least one complete sample calculation with units.

docs.title: Result
docs.text: | Line | Field formula verified | Slope obtained | Expected slope | |---|---|---|---|| | | | | |

docs.title: Precautions
docs.text:
• Always keep r ≫ 2a so the point-dipole approximation is valid; note the deviation if this is violated.
  • Track the sign of the potential carefully — it is positive on the +q side and negative on the −q side of the equatorial plane.
  • When sweeping θ, keep r fixed to isolate the angular dependence cleanly.
  • Distinguish clearly between the radial and tangential components of E when off-axis.

docs.title: Maximum Permissible Error
docs.text:
\[ ΔE/E = Δq/q + Δ(2a)/(2a) + 3Δr/r \]

E_axial = 2kp/r³ with p=q·(2a) varies as r⁻³, so its fractional uncertainty carries a factor of 3 from r.

docs.title: Viva-Voce Questions
docs.text:
1. Why does the dipole field fall off as 1/r³ while a single point charge's field falls off as 1/r²?
  2. Show that the potential on the equatorial line of a dipole is zero everywhere.
  3. What is the torque on a dipole placed in a uniform external field, and at what orientation is it maximum?
  4. Define dipole moment as a vector and state its SI unit.
  5. How would the field pattern change for a linear quadrupole (two dipoles placed back-to-back)?
  6. Explain the physical origin of polar molecules' permanent dipole moment (e.g. in water).

docs.title: Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction
docs.text: ← Back to BHP301 Course Page · Launch Simulator · 📝 Open Lab Record & Viva-Voce Booklet (PDF) (print just this experiment's pages when you sit down at the simulator)

docs.title: Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction
docs.text:
SOURCE / CONTROL APPARATUS (virtual setup) PROBE / DETECTOR A virtual coil linked to a bar magnet that can be moved at a controllable speed, plus an independently-adjustable external field source (for studying dB/dt with a stationary coil), with a galvanometer/oscilloscope trace of the induced EMF.

Apparatus schematic · A virtual coil linked to a bar magnet that can be moved at a controllable speed, plus an independently-adjustable external field source (for studying dB/dt with a stationary coil), with a galvanometer/oscilloscope trace of the induced EMF.

docs.title: Aim
docs.text: To verify Faraday's law of electromagnetic induction by studying the EMF induced in a coil due to a changing magnetic flux, and to confirm Lenz's law for the direction of the induced current.

docs.title: Theoretical Background
docs.text: Faraday's law states that the EMF induced in a circuit equals the negative rate of change of magnetic flux linkage through it: ε = −N·dΦ_B/dt, where N is the number of turns and Φ_B = ∫B·dA is the flux through one turn. The flux can change because the field itself varies in time, because the coil's area or orientation changes, or because a magnet moves relative to the coil. The negative sign is Lenz's law: the induced current always flows in the direction that opposes the change producing it, consistent with energy conservation — work must be done against the induced effects to change the flux, and that work is what appears as electrical energy in the circuit.

docs.title: Governing Formula
docs.text: \[ ε = −N dΦ_B/dt \] Symbol Meaning ε induced EMF (V) N number of turns in the coil Φ_B magnetic flux linking one turn (Wb) t time (s)

docs.title: Interactive Controls
docs.text:
Launch the simulator and use the following controls on the Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction panel:

Control Symbol Range Default Number of turns N 10–500 100 Magnet approach speed v 1–50 cm/s 10 cm/s Peak field of the magnet B_max 5–100 mT 30 mT Coil cross-sectional area A 5–50 cm² 20 cm²

docs.title: Procedure
docs.text:
1. Move the magnet toward the coil at a fixed speed and record the induced EMF pulse on the virtual oscilloscope; note its sign.
  2. Repeat, withdrawing the magnet at the same speed, and note the induced EMF reverses sign.
  3. Double the approach speed and verify the peak EMF approximately doubles (steeper dB/dt).
  4. Increase the number of turns N and verify the induced EMF scales proportionally.
  5. Hold the magnet stationary inside the coil and confirm the induced EMF is exactly zero (no change in flux).
  6. Use the induced current's direction (read from the galvanometer deflection) to verify Lenz's law by checking it opposes the magnet's approach/withdrawal.

docs.title: Precautions
docs.text:
• Keep the magnet's motion smooth and at a controlled, known speed to get a clean, repeatable EMF pulse.
  • Note carefully which face of the magnet (N or S pole) enters the coil first — it fixes the sign of the first EMF lobe.
  • Vary only one control (speed, turns, or field strength) at a time to isolate each dependence.
  • Remember a stationary magnet inside a stationary coil induces exactly zero EMF, however strong the field.

docs.title: Maximum Permissible Error
docs.text:
\[ Δε/ε = ΔN/N + ΔB_max/B_max + Δv/v \]

For a magnet swept through at speed v, peak EMF scales with N, the field's peak value and its rate of change (∝ v), so fractional errors add.

docs.title: Viva-Voce Questions
docs.text:
1. State Faraday's law and explain the physical meaning of the negative sign (Lenz's law).
  2. Why is no EMF induced by a strong but unchanging magnetic field through a stationary coil?
  3. Derive the motional EMF formula ε = BLv from Faraday's law for a rod sliding on rails in a magnetic field.
  4. Explain how Lenz's law is a direct consequence of energy conservation.
  5. How does eddy-current braking make use of Faraday's and Lenz's laws?
  6. What is self-inductance, and how does it relate to Faraday's law applied to a coil's own changing current?

docs.title: Force Between Parallel Conductors
docs.text: ← Back to BHP301 Course Page · Launch Simulator · 📝 Open Lab Record & Viva-Voce Booklet (PDF) (print just this experiment's pages when you sit down at the simulator)

docs.title: Force Between Two Parallel Current-Carrying Conductors
docs.text:
SOURCE / CONTROL APPARATUS (virtual setup) PROBE / DETECTOR Two virtual long parallel wires with independently adjustable currents (magnitude and direction) and separation, with a force sensor reporting the attractive or repulsive force per unit length.

Apparatus schematic · Two virtual long parallel wires with independently adjustable currents (magnitude and direction) and separation, with a force sensor reporting the attractive or repulsive force per unit length.

docs.title: Aim
docs.text: To compute the force per unit length between two long parallel current-carrying wires, verify its dependence on the currents and their separation, and relate it to the SI definition of the ampere.

docs.title: Theoretical Background
docs.text: Two long straight parallel wires carrying currents I₁ and I₂, separated by distance d, exert a magnetic force on each other because each wire's field acts on the other's current-carrying charges. The force per unit length is F/L = μ₀I₁I₂/(2πd). Currents in the same direction attract; currents in opposite directions repel — the reverse of the electrostatic case for like/unlike charges. This result was historically used to define the ampere itself: one ampere was (pre-2019) the constant current that, if maintained in each of two straight parallel infinite conductors of negligible cross-section placed one metre apart in vacuum, would produce a force of exactly 2×10⁻⁷ newtons per metre of length between them.

docs.title: Governing Formula
docs.text: \[ F/L = μ₀ I₁ I₂ / (2π d) \] Symbol Meaning F/L force per unit length between the wires (N/m) I₁, I₂ currents in the two wires (A) d separation between the wires (m) μ₀ permeability of free space

docs.title: Interactive Controls
docs.text:
Launch the simulator and use the following controls on the Force Between Two Parallel Current-Carrying Conductors panel:

Control Symbol Range Default Current in wire 1 I₁ 0.5–20 A 5 A Current in wire 2 I₂ 0.5–20 A 5 A Separation d 1–30 cm 5 cm Relative direction (0=same, 1=opposite) dir 0–1 0

docs.title: Procedure
docs.text:
1. Set both currents in the same direction and vary the separation d; plot F/L vs 1/d and verify linearity.
  2. Fix d and vary I₁ (keeping I₂ fixed); verify F/L ∝ I₁.
  3. Reverse the direction of I₂ and note the force changes from attractive to repulsive at the same magnitudes.
  4. Set I₁ = I₂ = 1 A and d = 1 m, and verify the simulator returns F/L = 2×10⁻⁷ N/m, the historical ampere-defining value.
  5. Increase both currents simultaneously (I₁ = I₂ = I) and verify F/L ∝ I².
  6. Estimate the force on a 1 m length of two household-wiring conductors carrying typical currents, and comment on why it is not noticeable in daily life.

docs.text:

docs.title: Precautions
docs.text:
• Keep track of current direction carefully — it determines attraction versus repulsion, not just magnitude.
  • Vary only one of I₁, I₂, d at a time to isolate each dependence cleanly.
  • Remember this is an idealization for infinitely long, thin wires; real finite wires show edge corrections.
  • Use consistent SI units throughout when checking the 2×10⁻⁷ N/m reference case.

docs.title: Maximum Permissible Error
docs.text:
\[ Δ(F/L)/(F/L) = ΔI₁/I₁ + ΔI₂/I₂ + Δd/d \]

F/L = μ₀I₁I₂/2πd is a product/quotient of three measured quantities.

docs.title: Viva-Voce Questions
docs.text:
1. Derive the force per unit length between two parallel current-carrying wires using the Biot–Savart/Ampere field of one wire acting on the other.
  2. Why do parallel currents in the same direction attract while antiparallel currents repel?
  3. Explain the historical (pre-2019) SI definition of the ampere in terms of this force.
  4. How does the modern (2019 revision) SI definition of the ampere differ, and why was it changed?
  5. If both currents are doubled, by what factor does the force per unit length change?
  6. How would you measure this force experimentally using a current balance?

docs.title: Gauss's Law — Electric Flux Through Closed Surfaces
docs.text: ← Back to BHP301 Course Page · Launch Simulator · 📝 Open Lab Record & Viva-Voce Booklet (PDF) (print just this experiment's pages when you sit down at the simulator)

docs.title: Gauss's Law — Electric Flux Through Closed Surfaces
docs.text:
SOURCE / CONTROL APPARATUS (virtual setup) PROBE / DETECTOR A virtual charge enclosed within a draggable/resizable Gaussian surface (sphere, cube or cylinder); the simulator numerically integrates E·dA over the chosen surface and displays the running flux total.

Apparatus schematic · A virtual charge enclosed within a draggable/resizable Gaussian surface (sphere, cube or cylinder); the simulator numerically integrates E·dA over the chosen surface and displays the running flux total.

docs.title: Aim
docs.text: To verify Gauss's law by computing the net electric flux through virtual closed Gaussian surfaces (sphere, cube, cylinder) enclosing a known charge, and to relate the flux to the field of standard symmetric charge distributions.

docs.title: Theoretical Background
docs.text: Gauss's law states that the net electric flux Φ_E through any closed surface equals the enclosed charge divided by ε₀: Φ_E = ∮ E·dA = Q_enc/ε₀. This holds regardless of the surface's shape and regardless of where the enclosed charge sits inside it, and charges outside the surface contribute zero net flux. For distributions with sufficient symmetry (spherical, cylindrical, planar), Gauss's law lets the field be obtained directly without integrating Coulomb's law: a spherical shell of charge Q gives E = kQ/r² outside and E = 0 inside; an infinite line charge of linear density λ gives E = λ/(2πε₀ρ); an infinite charged sheet of surface density σ gives a uniform field E = σ/(2ε₀) independent of distance from the sheet.

docs.title: Governing Formula
docs.text: \[ Φ_E = ∮ E·dA = Q_enc / ε₀ \] Symbol Meaning Φ_E net electric flux through the closed surface (N·m²/C) Q_enc total charge enclosed by the surface (C) ε₀ permittivity of free space

docs.title: Interactive Controls
docs.text:
Launch the simulator and use the following controls on the Gauss's Law — Electric Flux Through Closed Surfaces panel:

Control Symbol Range Default Enclosed charge Q_enc -20–20 nC 10 nC Charge placed outside surface Q_out -20–20 nC 5 nC Gaussian sphere radius R 5–40 cm 15 cm Surface shape shape – sphere

docs.title: Procedure
docs.text:
1. Place a known charge Q_enc at the centre of a spherical Gaussian surface of radius R and record the computed flux.
  2. Change R (keeping Q_enc fixed) and verify the flux is independent of the radius chosen.
  3. Move Q_enc off-centre within the same surface and confirm the total flux is unchanged.
  4. Add a second charge Q_out entirely outside the surface and verify it contributes zero net flux, even though it changes the local field at the surface.
  5. Switch the Gaussian surface shape from sphere to cube to cylinder (same enclosed charge) and confirm the flux total is identical in every case.
  6. Use the flux value to back-calculate E for a spherical shell and compare with the Coulomb's-law field at the same radius.

docs.title: Graph
docs.text:
Field of a Charged Sphere: E vs r — E=0 inside, E∝1/r² outside

📋 Click the grid, then paste (Ctrl+V) a copied photo of your reading/meter trace to overlay it for tracing — nothing is uploaded, it stays on your screen only. Overlay opacity Clear pasted image Field of a Charged Sphere: E vs r r (cm) → E (relative units) → 15×15 grid — choose your own scale per square; plot from your observation table. Expected trend: E=0 inside, E∝1/r² outside

docs.title: Calculations
docs.text: Use the governing formula above with your tabulated observations to compute the required result for each row. Show at least one complete sample calculation with units.

docs.title: Result
docs.text: | Case | Flux measured | Flux predicted (Q_enc/ε₀) | % deviation | |---|---|---|---|| | | | | |

docs.title: Precautions
docs.text:
• Make sure the chosen surface is genuinely closed (no gaps) before reading off the flux, or the numerical integration will be inaccurate.
  • Distinguish carefully between charges enclosed by the surface and charges merely near it but outside.
  • For the cylindrical surface, remember to include the flux through the flat end-caps as well as the curved side.
  • Keep the enclosed charge exactly at stated positions when comparing symmetric vs asymmetric placements.

docs.title: Maximum Permissible Error
docs.text:
\[ ΔΦ/Φ = ΔQ_enc/Q_enc \]

Since Φ = Q_enc/ε₀ with ε₀ an exact constant, the fractional error in flux equals the fractional error in the enclosed charge measurement.

docs.title: Viva-Voce Questions
docs.text:
1. State Gauss's law and explain why it is a direct consequence of Coulomb's law and the inverse-square dependence.
  2. Why is Gauss's law most useful only for highly symmetric charge distributions?
  3. Show that the field inside a uniformly charged spherical shell is exactly zero.
  4. Derive the field of an infinite charged sheet using Gauss's law with a pillbox surface.
  5. If a charge lies exactly on the Gaussian surface, how should it be treated?
  6. How does Gauss's law extend to Gauss's law for magnetism (∇·B = 0), and what does that imply physically?

docs.title: LCR Series Resonance & Q-factor
docs.text: ← Back to BHP301 Course Page · Launch Simulator · 📝 Open Lab Record & Viva-Voce Booklet (PDF) (print just this experiment's pages when you sit down at the simulator)

docs.title: LCR Series Resonance & Quality Factor (Q)
docs.text:
SOURCE / CONTROL APPARATUS (virtual setup) PROBE / DETECTOR A virtual series LCR circuit driven by a variable-frequency AC source, with adjustable R, L, C and a live current-versus- frequency plot showing the resonance peak.

Apparatus schematic · A virtual series LCR circuit driven by a variable-frequency AC source, with adjustable R, L, C and a live current-versus-frequency plot showing the resonance peak.

docs.title: Aim
docs.text: To study the resonance behaviour of a series LCR circuit driven by an AC source, locate the resonant frequency, and determine the quality factor from the sharpness of the resonance curve.

docs.title: Theoretical Background
docs.text: In a series LCR circuit driven by a sinusoidal source of angular frequency ω, the impedance is Z = √[R² + (ωL − 1/ωC)²]. The current amplitude I(ω) = V₀/Z is maximum when the reactive term vanishes, at the resonant angular frequency ω₀ = 1/√(LC), where the impedance reduces to purely R and the circuit behaves resistively with the largest possible current. The sharpness of the resonance peak is characterised by the quality factor Q = ω₀L/R = (1/R)√(L/C) = ω₀/Δω, where Δω is the full width of the resonance curve at half the maximum power (equivalently, at I = I_max/√2). A higher Q means a lower R relative to √(L/C), giving a narrower, taller resonance peak and better frequency selectivity, as exploited in radio tuning circuits.

docs.title: Governing Formula
docs.text: \[ ω₀ = 1/√(LC),   Q = ω₀L/R = (1/R)√(L/C) \] Symbol Meaning ω₀ resonant angular frequency (rad/s) L inductance (H) C capacitance (F) R resistance (Ω) Q quality factor (dimensionless)

docs.title: Interactive Controls
docs.text:
Launch the simulator and use the following controls on the LCR Series Resonance & Quality Factor (Q) panel:

Control Symbol Range Default Resistance R 1–200 Ω 20 Ω Inductance L 1–500 mH 100 mH Capacitance C 0.1–10 μF 1 μF Source amplitude V₀ 1–20 V 10 V Drive frequency f 10–5000 Hz 500 Hz

docs.title: Procedure
docs.text:
1. Sweep the drive frequency f across a wide range and plot the current amplitude I(f); locate the peak and compare with f₀ = 1/(2π√LC).
  2. Read off the half-power frequencies (where I = I_max/√2) and compute the bandwidth Δf; hence determine Q = f₀/Δf.
  3. Increase R (keeping L, C fixed) and observe the resonance peak becoming shorter and broader; verify Q decreases as expected.
  4. Vary L and C while keeping their product LC fixed (so f₀ is unchanged) and observe how the peak height and width change with √(L/C).
  5. At resonance, verify that the voltage across L and across C individually can each exceed the source voltage V₀, while remaining 180° out of phase with each other.
  6. Compute Q by two independent methods — from the bandwidth, and from Q = ω₀L/R — and compare.

docs.title: Graph
docs.text:
Resonance Curve: Current vs Frequency — peak at f₀; width sets Q

📋 Click the grid, then paste (Ctrl+V) a copied photo of your reading/meter trace to overlay it for tracing — nothing is uploaded, it stays on your screen only. Overlay opacity Clear pasted image Resonance Curve: Current vs Frequency f (Hz) → I (mA) → 15×15 grid — choose your own scale per square; plot from your observation table. Expected trend: peak at f₀; width sets Q

docs.title: Calculations
docs.text: Use the governing formula above with your tabulated observations to compute the required result for each row. Show at least one complete sample calculation with units.

docs.title: Result
docs.text: | Quantity | Measured value | Calculated value | % deviation | |---|---|---|---|| | | | | |

docs.title: Precautions
docs.text:
• Sweep frequency finely (small steps) near resonance, since the peak can be narrow for high-Q circuits.
  • Keep the source amplitude V₀ fixed throughout a frequency sweep.
  • Vary only one of R, L, C at a time when studying their individual effects on the resonance curve.
  • Remember the individual voltages across L and C can exceed the source voltage at resonance — this is expected, not an error.

docs.title: Maximum Permissible Error
docs.text:
\[ Δf₀/f₀ = ½(ΔL/L + ΔC/C) \]

f₀ = 1/(2π√LC) involves a square root of the product LC, halving the summed fractional errors.

docs.title: Viva-Voce Questions
docs.text:
1. Derive the resonant frequency of a series LCR circuit from its impedance expression.
  2. Define quality factor Q in at least two equivalent ways and show they are consistent.
  3. Why can the voltage across the inductor or capacitor individually exceed the applied source voltage at resonance?
  4. How does increasing R affect the sharpness and height of the resonance curve?
  5. Compare series and parallel LCR resonance — which one gives minimum impedance and which gives maximum impedance at resonance?
  6. Explain how this resonance principle is used in radio-frequency tuning circuits.

docs.title: Magnetic Hysteresis & the B–H Curve
docs.text: ← Back to BHP301 Course Page · Launch Simulator · 📝 Open Lab Record & Viva-Voce Booklet (PDF) (print just this experiment's pages when you sit down at the simulator)

docs.title: Magnetic Hysteresis & the B–H Curve of Ferromagnetic Materials
docs.text:
SOURCE / CONTROL APPARATUS (virtual setup) PROBE / DETECTOR A virtual toroidal sample of selectable ferromagnetic material (soft iron, hard steel, ferrite) wound with a magnetizing coil whose current (hence H) can be cycled, with a live B–H plotter tracing out the hysteresis loop and reporting B_sat, B_r, H_c and loop area.

Apparatus schematic · A virtual toroidal sample of selectable ferromagnetic material (soft iron, hard steel, ferrite) wound with a magnetizing coil whose current (hence H) can be cycled, with a live B–H plotter tracing out the hysteresis loop and reporting B_sat, B_r, H_c and loop area.

docs.title: Aim
docs.text: To trace the B–H hysteresis loop of a ferromagnetic material as the applied field H is cycled, and to determine the saturation magnetization, retentivity (remanence), coercivity, and hysteresis energy loss per cycle.

docs.title: Theoretical Background
docs.text: In a ferromagnetic material, the magnetic flux density B does not respond linearly or reversibly to the applied field H; instead it traces a hysteresis loop B(H) that depends on the material's magnetic history. Starting from an unmagnetized sample and increasing H, B rises along an initial magnetization curve toward saturation B_sat, where nearly all magnetic domains have aligned with the field. Reducing H back to zero leaves a residual flux density called the retentivity (remanence) B_r. Reversing H further, B falls to zero only at a negative field called the coercivity −H_c, the field needed to fully demagnetize the sample. Continuing the cycle traces the full closed loop. The area enclosed by the B–H loop equals the energy dissipated as heat per unit volume per cycle (hysteresis loss), due to irreversible domain-wall motion — which is why transformer cores use 'soft' magnetic materials with narrow loops (low B_r, low H_c, small area) while permanent magnets use 'hard' materials with wide loops (high B_r, high H_c).

docs.title: Governing Formula
docs.text: \[ H = nI,   Hysteresis loss per cycle per unit volume = ∮ H dB (loop area) \] Symbol Meaning H magnetizing field (A/m) B magnetic flux density in the material (T) B_r retentivity / remanence (T) H_c coercivity (A/m) B_sat saturation flux density (T)

docs.title: Interactive Controls
docs.text:
Launch the simulator and use the following controls on the Magnetic Hysteresis & the B–H Curve of Ferromagnetic Materials panel:

Control Symbol Range Default Material (0=soft iron, 1=ferrite, 2=hard steel) mat 0–2 0 Peak applied field H_max 50–2000 A/m 500 A/m Number of cycles to trace n 1–5 1

docs.title: Procedure
docs.text:
1. Starting from an unmagnetized (virgin) sample, increase H from 0 to H_max and trace the initial magnetization curve up to saturation.
  2. Cycle H from +H_max to −H_max and back, tracing the full closed hysteresis loop; read off B_r (at H=0) and H_c (at B=0) from the plot.
  3. Repeat for a 'soft' material (small loop) and a 'hard' material (wide loop) and compare B_r, H_c and loop areas.
  4. Estimate the hysteresis energy loss per cycle per unit volume by numerically computing the enclosed loop area, ∮H dB.
  5. Reduce H_max below saturation and observe a smaller 'minor loop' traced inside the full major loop.
  6. Relate the measured loop shape to a suitable application: which material would you choose for a transformer core, and which for a permanent magnet, and why?

docs.title: Graph
docs.text:
B–H Hysteresis Loop — closed loop; area = energy loss/cycle

📋 Click the grid, then paste (Ctrl+V) a copied photo of your reading/meter trace to overlay it for tracing — nothing is uploaded, it stays on your screen only. Overlay opacity Clear pasted image B–H Hysteresis Loop H (A/m) → B (T) → 15×15 grid — choose your own scale per square; plot from your observation table. Expected trend: closed loop; area = energy loss/cycle

docs.title: Calculations
docs.text: Use the governing formula above with your tabulated observations to compute the required result for each row. Show at least one complete sample calculation with units.

docs.title: Result
docs.text: | Material | B_sat (T) | Retentivity B_r (T) | Coercivity H_c (A/m) | Loop area / cycle loss | |---|---|---|---|---|| | | | | | |

docs.title: Precautions
docs.text:
• Always begin from a genuinely demagnetized (virgin) sample when tracing the initial magnetization curve, or the starting point of the loop will be ambiguous.
  • Cycle H smoothly through its full range without reversing direction partway (except deliberately, to study minor loops).
  • Distinguish clearly between retentivity (B at H=0) and coercivity (H at B=0) — a common source of confusion.
  • Note that loop area (hysteresis loss) has units of energy per unit volume per cycle, not power — multiply by frequency to get power loss.

docs.title: Maximum Permissible Error
docs.text:
\[ H_c ≈ 0.15×H_max (model estimate); ΔH_c/H_c = ΔH_max/H_max \]

In this model, coercivity is estimated as a fixed fraction of the peak applied field; its uncertainty tracks the peak-field setting's fractional uncertainty.

docs.title: Viva-Voce Questions
docs.text:
1. Define retentivity and coercivity, and explain how each is read off a B–H loop.
  2. Why does the area enclosed by the hysteresis loop represent energy dissipated as heat?
  3. Distinguish between magnetically 'soft' and 'hard' materials in terms of their hysteresis loops, and give one application of each.
  4. Why do transformer cores use soft magnetic materials with narrow loops rather than hard materials?
  5. What is a domain, and how does domain-wall motion explain the irreversibility seen in hysteresis?
  6. How does the hysteresis loss per cycle relate to the total power loss in an AC-excited transformer core operating at 50 Hz?
