docs.title: Maxwell's Equations & Plane Waves
docs.text: ← Back to BHP301 Course Page · Launch Simulator · 📝 Open Lab Record & Viva-Voce Booklet (PDF) (print just this experiment's pages when you sit down at the simulator)

docs.title: Maxwell's Equations & Plane Electromagnetic Wave Solutions
docs.text:
SOURCE / CONTROL APPARATUS (virtual setup) PROBE / DETECTOR A virtual 3-D animated E and B field visualiser for a plane wave propagating along z, with adjustable frequency and amplitude, and a toggle to independently vary E-field and B-field phase/amplitude to see how the wave 'breaks' if Maxwell's relations are violated.

Apparatus schematic · A virtual 3-D animated E and B field visualiser for a plane wave propagating along z, with adjustable frequency and amplitude, and a toggle to independently vary E-field and B-field phase/amplitude to see how the wave 'breaks' if Maxwell's relations are violated.

docs.title: Aim
docs.text: To explore how Maxwell's four equations, together, predict self-sustaining transverse electromagnetic waves in free space, and to verify the relations between E, B, their phase, and the speed of light.

docs.title: Theoretical Background
docs.text: Maxwell's equations in free space (no charges or currents) are ∇·E = 0, ∇·B = 0, ∇×E = −∂B/∂t, and ∇×B = μ₀ε₀∂E/∂t. Combining the curl equations by taking the curl of one and substituting the other yields a wave equation ∇²E = μ₀ε₀∂²E/∂t² (and similarly for B), whose solutions are transverse waves travelling at speed c = 1/√(μ₀ε₀) ≈ 3×10⁸ m/s. For a plane wave travelling along z, E = E₀ sin(kz−ωt) x̂ and B = B₀ sin(kz−ωt) ŷ, with E and B mutually perpendicular, both perpendicular to the direction of propagation, oscillating exactly in phase, and related in magnitude by B₀ = E₀/c at every instant. This is the theoretical foundation for all of classical optics, radio, and wireless communication.

docs.title: Governing Formula
docs.text: \[ c = 1/√(μ₀ε₀),   E₀/B₀ = c,   E ⊥ B ⊥ direction of propagation \] Symbol Meaning c speed of light in vacuum (m/s) μ₀ permeability of free space ε₀ permittivity of free space E₀, B₀ peak amplitudes of the electric and magnetic fields

docs.title: Interactive Controls
docs.text:
Launch the simulator and use the following controls on the Maxwell's Equations & Plane Electromagnetic Wave Solutions panel:

Control Symbol Range Default Wave frequency f 1–1000 MHz 100 MHz Electric field amplitude E₀ 1–100 V/m 30 V/m Artificial E-B phase offset (should be 0 physically) Δφ 0–90 ° 0 °

docs.title: Procedure
docs.text:
1. Set the phase offset to zero and observe E and B oscillating exactly in phase, perpendicular to each other and to the propagation direction.
  2. Read off B₀ for a chosen E₀ and verify B₀ = E₀/c.
  3. Increase the frequency and verify the wavelength λ = c/f shrinks proportionally while the wave speed itself stays fixed at c.
  4. Artificially introduce a phase offset between E and B using the slider and note this is not a physically realizable free-space plane wave (illustrating why Maxwell's equations force them in phase).
  5. Verify ∇·E = 0 for the plane wave by checking E has no component along its own direction of travel (purely transverse).
  6. Compute the Poynting vector S = E×B/μ₀ direction and confirm it points along the propagation direction, carrying energy forward.

docs.title: Graph
docs.text:
E and B Field Snapshot vs Position — E and B in phase, 90° apart in space

📋 Click the grid, then paste (Ctrl+V) a copied photo of your reading/meter trace to overlay it for tracing — nothing is uploaded, it stays on your screen only. Overlay opacity Clear pasted image E and B Field Snapshot vs Position z (m) → Field (normalized) → 15×15 grid — choose your own scale per square; plot from your observation table. Expected trend: E and B in phase, 90° apart in space

docs.title: Calculations
docs.text: Use the governing formula above with your tabulated observations to compute the required result for each row. Show at least one complete sample calculation with units.

docs.title: Result
docs.text: | Quantity | Measured value | Theoretical value (c) | % deviation | |---|---|---|---|| | | | | |

docs.title: Precautions
docs.text:
• Keep the phase-offset slider at zero except when specifically studying the (unphysical) mismatched case.
  • Remember E₀/B₀ = c only in SI units with E in V/m and B in tesla — check units carefully when verifying numerically.
  • Note that both E and B carry energy; the total energy density is shared equally between them in vacuum.
  • Distinguish the wave's phase velocity (always c in vacuum) from the frequency, which is set by the source.

docs.title: Maximum Permissible Error
docs.text:
\[ ΔB₀/B₀ = ΔE₀/E₀ \]

Since B₀ = E₀/c with c an exact constant, the fractional error in B₀ equals the fractional error in the measured E₀.

docs.title: Viva-Voce Questions
docs.text:
1. Starting from Maxwell's curl equations, outline the steps to derive the electromagnetic wave equation.
  2. Why must E and B be mutually perpendicular and both transverse to the direction of propagation?
  3. Show dimensionally that 1/√(μ₀ε₀) has the units of speed.
  4. What physical role does Maxwell's displacement current term play in predicting wave propagation?
  5. How is the Poynting vector defined, and what does it represent physically?
  6. Why was the prediction of electromagnetic waves by Maxwell considered one of the great unifications in physics?

docs.title: Motional EMF & Lenz's Law
docs.text: ← Back to BHP301 Course Page · Launch Simulator · 📝 Open Lab Record & Viva-Voce Booklet (PDF) (print just this experiment's pages when you sit down at the simulator)

docs.title: Motional EMF & Lenz's Law on a Sliding Rod
docs.text:
SOURCE / CONTROL APPARATUS (virtual setup) PROBE / DETECTOR A virtual pair of frictionless conducting rails of adjustable separation L, in a uniform field B, connected to a resistor R, with a rod that can be pushed at a controlled velocity v and instrumented for induced EMF, current, and retarding force.

Apparatus schematic · A virtual pair of frictionless conducting rails of adjustable separation L, in a uniform field B, connected to a resistor R, with a rod that can be pushed at a controlled velocity v and instrumented for induced EMF, current, and retarding force.

docs.title: Aim
docs.text: To study the motional EMF generated by a conducting rod sliding on rails in a uniform magnetic field, verify ε = BLv, and observe the retarding (Lenz's-law) force that opposes the rod's motion when the circuit is closed.

docs.title: Theoretical Background
docs.text: When a conducting rod of length L slides with velocity v perpendicular to both its length and a uniform magnetic field B, the free charges in the rod experience a magnetic force qv×B that drives them along the rod, building up an EMF ε = BLv (equivalently derivable from Faraday's law applied to the changing area swept out). If the rod completes a circuit of resistance R, a current I = ε/R flows, and this current in the field experiences a retarding force F = BIL that, by Lenz's law, always opposes the rod's motion — so an external agent must do work against this force to keep the rod moving at constant velocity, and that work exactly equals the electrical energy dissipated in R, consistent with energy conservation.

docs.title: Governing Formula
docs.text: \[ ε = BLv,   I = ε/R,   F_retard = BIL = B²L²v/R \] Symbol Meaning ε motional EMF (V) B magnetic field (T) L rail separation / rod length (m) v rod velocity (m/s) R circuit resistance (Ω) F_retard retarding force on the rod (N)

docs.title: Interactive Controls
docs.text:
Launch the simulator and use the following controls on the Motional EMF & Lenz's Law on a Sliding Rod panel:

Control Symbol Range Default Magnetic field B 10–500 mT 100 mT Rail separation L 5–40 cm 20 cm Rod velocity v 0.1–5 m/s 1 m/s Circuit resistance R 1–50 Ω 10 Ω

docs.title: Procedure
docs.text:
1. With the circuit open (infinite R), push the rod at various speeds and confirm the measured EMF matches ε = BLv with no retarding force (no current flows).
  2. Close the circuit through R and repeat; verify the current I = ε/R and observe the retarding force appearing.
  3. At fixed v, vary R and verify the retarding force decreases as R increases (F ∝ 1/R).
  4. At fixed R, vary B and verify the retarding force grows as B² (both the EMF and current scale with B).
  5. Push the rod at constant velocity against the retarding force and verify the input mechanical power equals the electrical power I²R dissipated in the resistor.
  6. Reverse the direction of the rod's motion and confirm both the EMF polarity and the retarding force direction reverse consistently with Lenz's law.

docs.title: Graph
docs.text:
Motional EMF vs Rod Velocity — straight line through origin, slope = BL

📋 Click the grid, then paste (Ctrl+V) a copied photo of your reading/meter trace to overlay it for tracing — nothing is uploaded, it stays on your screen only. Overlay opacity Clear pasted image Motional EMF vs Rod Velocity v (m/s) → ε (mV) → 15×15 grid — choose your own scale per square; plot from your observation table. Expected trend: straight line through origin, slope = BL

docs.title: Calculations
docs.text: Use the governing formula above with your tabulated observations to compute the required result for each row. Show at least one complete sample calculation with units.

docs.title: Result
docs.text: | Quantity | Measured value | Predicted value | % deviation | |---|---|---|---|| | | | | |

docs.title: Precautions
docs.text:
• Ensure the rod remains perpendicular to both the rails and the field direction throughout its motion.
  • Keep the rails frictionless and resistanceless in the model so only R limits the current, isolating the physics of interest.
  • Vary only one parameter (B, L, v, or R) at a time when tabulating results.
  • Note the retarding force acts only while the circuit is closed and current is flowing — an open circuit gives EMF but no force.

docs.title: Maximum Permissible Error
docs.text:
\[ Δε/ε = ΔB/B + ΔL/L + Δv/v \]

ε = BLv is a simple product of three measured quantities.

docs.title: Viva-Voce Questions
docs.text:
1. Derive the motional EMF ε = BLv from the magnetic force on free charges in the moving rod.
  2. Show that the same result follows from Faraday's law applied to the changing enclosed area.
  3. Why does the rod experience a retarding force only when the circuit is closed?
  4. Show that the electrical power dissipated equals the mechanical power delivered to keep the rod moving at constant velocity.
  5. How would the terminal (constant) velocity of a freely falling conducting rod on vertical rails in a field be determined?
  6. Explain the connection between this experiment and the working principle of an AC generator.

Author & Maintainer: Dr. S. K. Jain, Associate Professor in Physics, Department of Applied Sciences and Humanities, Invertis University, Bareilly, India 📧 sanjeev.j@invertis.org

docs.title: Capacitance of a Parallel-Plate Capacitor
docs.text: ← Back to BHP301 Course Page · Launch Simulator · 📝 Open Lab Record & Viva-Voce Booklet (PDF) (print just this experiment's pages when you sit down at the simulator)

docs.title: Capacitance of a Parallel-Plate Capacitor (With & Without Dielectric)
docs.text:
SOURCE / CONTROL APPARATUS (virtual setup) PROBE / DETECTOR A virtual parallel-plate capacitor with adjustable plate area and separation, a slot for inserting dielectric slabs of selectable material (air, paper, mica, glass, water), and a digital capacitance readout.

Apparatus schematic · A virtual parallel-plate capacitor with adjustable plate area and separation, a slot for inserting dielectric slabs of selectable material (air, paper, mica, glass, water), and a digital capacitance readout.

docs.title: Aim
docs.text: To study the dependence of a parallel-plate capacitor's capacitance on plate area, plate separation, and an inserted dielectric slab, and to determine the dielectric constant of an unknown material.

docs.title: Theoretical Background
docs.text: A parallel-plate capacitor of plate area A separated by distance d in vacuum has capacitance C₀ = ε₀A/d. Filling the gap with a dielectric of relative permittivity εr increases the capacitance to C = εrε₀A/d = εr·C₀, because the bound polarization charges on the dielectric surfaces partially cancel the free-charge field, lowering the potential difference for the same stored charge. If the dielectric slab of thickness t < d only partially fills the gap, the capacitor behaves as two capacitors in series: C = ε₀A / [d − t(1 − 1/εr)]. The stored energy is U = Q²/2C = ½CV², and inserting a dielectric while the plates remain connected to a battery (constant V) increases the stored charge and energy, while at constant charge Q it lowers the voltage and the energy.

docs.title: Governing Formula
docs.text: \[ C = εrε₀A/d \] Symbol Meaning C capacitance (F) εr relative permittivity (dielectric constant) of the medium A overlap area of the plates (m²) d plate separation (m)

docs.title: Interactive Controls
docs.text:
Launch the simulator and use the following controls on the Capacitance of a Parallel-Plate Capacitor (With & Without Dielectric) panel:

Control Symbol Range Default Plate area A 50–500 cm² 200 cm² Plate separation d 0.5–10 mm 2 mm Dielectric constant εr 1–80 1 Applied voltage V 1–100 V 10 V

docs.title: Procedure
docs.text:
1. With air between the plates (εr = 1), vary the separation d and plot C vs 1/d to verify the linear relationship.
  2. Fix d and vary the plate area A; plot C vs A and verify a straight line through the origin.
  3. Insert dielectric slabs of known εr one at a time (paper ≈ 3, mica ≈ 6, glass ≈ 5–7) fully filling the gap and record the new capacitance; compute εr = C/C₀ for each.
  4. Use an 'unknown' slab and determine its dielectric constant from the capacitance ratio.
  5. At fixed Q, insert the dielectric and observe the voltage drop; at fixed V (battery connected), insert the dielectric and observe the increase in stored charge.
  6. Compute and compare the stored energy U before and after dielectric insertion in both the constant-Q and constant-V cases.

docs.title: Graph
docs.text:
Capacitance vs 1/d — straight line through origin

📋 Click the grid, then paste (Ctrl+V) a copied photo of your reading/meter trace to overlay it for tracing — nothing is uploaded, it stays on your screen only. Overlay opacity Clear pasted image Capacitance vs 1/d 1/d (mm⁻¹) → C (pF) → 15×15 grid — choose your own scale per square; plot from your observation table. Expected trend: straight line through origin

docs.title: Calculations
docs.text: Use the governing formula above with your tabulated observations to compute the required result for each row. Show at least one complete sample calculation with units.

docs.title: Result
docs.text: | Dielectric material | Measured εr = C/C₀ | Known/standard εr | % deviation | |---|---|---|---|| | | | | |

docs.title: Precautions
docs.text:
• Ensure the dielectric slab fully and uniformly fills the gap between the plates unless deliberately studying partial filling.
  • Keep the plate area and dielectric fixed while varying only the separation, and vice versa.
  • Avoid short-circuiting the plates (d → 0); maintain a realistic minimum separation.
  • Discharge the capacitor fully before changing the dielectric or geometry to avoid stray residual charge.

docs.title: Maximum Permissible Error
docs.text:
\[ ΔC/C = Δεr/εr + ΔA/A + Δd/d \]

C = εrε₀A/d is a product/quotient of three measured quantities, so fractional errors add.

docs.title: Viva-Voce Questions
docs.text:
1. Derive the capacitance of a parallel-plate capacitor from first principles using Gauss's law.
  2. Why does inserting a dielectric always increase the capacitance, never decrease it?
  3. Distinguish between the behaviour of a dielectric slab at constant charge versus constant voltage.
  4. Define dielectric constant (relative permittivity) and polarization P; how are they related?
  5. What is dielectric breakdown, and what determines a material's dielectric strength?
  6. How would you find the capacitance of a capacitor with the gap only half-filled by a dielectric slab parallel to the plates?

docs.text: ← Back to BHP301 Course Page · Launch Simulator · 📝 Open Lab Record & Viva-Voce Booklet (PDF) (print just this experiment's pages when you sit down at the simulator)

docs.title: RC and RL Transient Circuits — Charging & Discharging
docs.text:
SOURCE / CONTROL APPARATUS (virtual setup) PROBE / DETECTOR A virtual series RC circuit and a series RL circuit, each with a switch to connect/disconnect a battery, adjustable R, C (or L), and a virtual oscilloscope tracing the voltage/current transient in real time with cursors to measure τ.

Apparatus schematic · A virtual series RC circuit and a series RL circuit, each with a switch to connect/disconnect a battery, adjustable R, C (or L), and a virtual oscilloscope tracing the voltage/current transient in real time with cursors to measure τ.

docs.title: Aim
docs.text: To study the exponential charging and discharging behaviour of RC and RL circuits, measure the time constant in each case, and verify its dependence on the circuit parameters.

docs.title: Theoretical Background
docs.text: In a series RC circuit charged through a resistor R from a battery of EMF ε₀, the capacitor voltage grows as V_C(t) = ε₀(1 − e^{−t/τ}) with time constant τ = RC, and discharges as V_C(t) = V₀ e^{−t/τ} once the battery is removed and the capacitor is shorted through R. Analogously, in a series RL circuit the current grows as I(t) = (ε₀/R)(1 − e^{−t/τ}) with time constant τ = L/R, and decays as I(t) = I₀ e^{−t/τ} when the source is removed. In both cases τ is the time for the quantity to reach (1 − 1/e) ≈ 63.2% of its final value while charging, or to fall to 1/e ≈ 36.8% of its initial value while discharging, and after about 5τ the transient is essentially complete.

docs.title: Governing Formula
docs.text: \[ RC: V_C(t) = ε₀(1−e^{-t/τ}), τ=RC     |     RL: I(t) = (ε₀/R)(1−e^{-t/τ}), τ=L/R \] Symbol Meaning τ time constant of the circuit (s) R resistance (Ω) C capacitance (F) L inductance (H) ε₀ source EMF (V)

docs.title: Interactive Controls
docs.text:
Launch the simulator and use the following controls on the RC and RL Transient Circuits — Charging & Discharging panel:

Control Symbol Range Default Resistance R 1–100 kΩ 10 kΩ Capacitance C 1–100 μF 10 μF Inductance (RL mode) L 0.1–10 H 1 H Source EMF ε₀ 1–20 V 10 V Circuit (0=RC, 1=RL) mode 0–1 0

docs.title: Procedure
docs.text:
1. For the RC circuit, close the switch and record V_C(t) on the oscilloscope; read off the time to reach 63.2% of ε₀ and compare with RC.
  2. Vary R (keeping C fixed) and verify τ scales proportionally; repeat varying C at fixed R.
  3. Discharge the capacitor through R and verify the decay time constant matches the charging time constant.
  4. Switch to the RL circuit and repeat: measure the growth time constant and verify τ = L/R.
  5. Vary L and R independently for the RL circuit and verify the corresponding scaling of τ.
  6. Compare the general shapes of the RC-charging and RL-growth curves and note they are functionally identical apart from which quantity (voltage vs current) is plotted.

docs.title: Graph
docs.text:
Capacitor Charging Curve — 63.2% at t=τ, ~99% at t=5τ

📋 Click the grid, then paste (Ctrl+V) a copied photo of your reading/meter trace to overlay it for tracing — nothing is uploaded, it stays on your screen only. Overlay opacity Clear pasted image Capacitor Charging Curve t/τ → V_C/ε₀ → 15×15 grid — choose your own scale per square; plot from your observation table. Expected trend: 63.2% at t=τ, ~99% at t=5τ

docs.title: Calculations
docs.text: Use the governing formula above with your tabulated observations to compute the required result for each row. Show at least one complete sample calculation with units.

docs.title: Result
docs.text: | Circuit | τ measured | τ calculated | % deviation | |---|---|---|---|| | | | | |

docs.title: Precautions
docs.text:
• Allow at least 5τ for the transient to settle before considering the circuit to be at steady state.
  • Discharge the capacitor completely (or de-energize the inductor) before starting a fresh charging run.
  • Vary only R or only C (or only L) between successive runs to isolate each dependence.
  • Remember an ideal inductor's current cannot change instantaneously, and an ideal capacitor's voltage cannot change instantaneously — use this as a sanity check on the simulated traces.

docs.title: Maximum Permissible Error
docs.text:
\[ Δτ/τ = ΔR/R + ΔC/C (RC circuit) \]

τ = RC is a simple product, so fractional errors in R and C add directly.

docs.title: Viva-Voce Questions
docs.text:
1. Derive the charging equation of an RC circuit from Kirchhoff's voltage law.
  2. What is the physical significance of the time constant τ, and how is it read off an exponential curve graphically?
  3. Why do the RC-charging and RL-growth curves have the same mathematical form?
  4. How much energy is ultimately stored in the capacitor after full charging, and how does this compare with the energy dissipated in R during charging?
  5. What happens to the time constant if R is doubled while C is halved?
  6. Explain the duality between RC and RL transient circuits in terms of the roles of voltage and current.

Author & Maintainer: Dr. S. K. Jain, Associate Professor in Physics, Department of Applied Sciences and Humanities, Invertis University, Bareilly, India 📧 sanjeev.j@invertis.org

docs.title: Reflection, Refraction & Brewster's Angle
docs.text: ← Back to BHP301 Course Page · Launch Simulator · 📝 Open Lab Record & Viva-Voce Booklet (PDF) (print just this experiment's pages when you sit down at the simulator)

docs.title: Reflection, Refraction & Brewster's Angle for EM Waves
docs.text:
SOURCE / CONTROL APPARATUS (virtual setup) PROBE / DETECTOR A virtual ray-optics bench with an EM wave incident on a dielectric interface of adjustable n₁, n₂, with adjustable incidence angle and selectable s- or p-polarization, showing computed reflectance/transmittance and the reflected ray's polarization state.

Apparatus schematic · A virtual ray-optics bench with an EM wave incident on a dielectric interface of adjustable n₁, n₂, with adjustable incidence angle and selectable s- or p-polarization, showing computed reflectance/transmittance and the reflected ray's polarization state.

docs.title: Aim
docs.text: To study the reflection and transmission of an electromagnetic wave at a dielectric interface using the Fresnel equations, and to locate Brewster's angle at which p-polarized reflection vanishes.

docs.title: Theoretical Background
docs.text: When an EM wave meets the planar interface between two dielectric media of refractive indices n₁ and n₂, part of it reflects and part refracts according to Snell's law n₁sinθᵢ = n₂sinθₜ. The fraction reflected depends on polarization and angle of incidence, given by the Fresnel equations. For light polarized with its E-field in the plane of incidence (p-polarization), the reflection coefficient passes through exactly zero at a special angle called Brewster's angle, θ_B = arctan(n₂/n₁), at which the reflected and refracted rays are perpendicular to each other. At this angle, only s-polarized light is reflected, so reflected light off a dielectric surface at Brewster's angle is completely (linearly) polarized — the principle exploited by polarizing sunglasses and by Brewster windows in laser cavities to eliminate reflection losses for one polarization.

docs.title: Governing Formula
docs.text: \[ Snell's law: n₁ sinθᵢ = n₂ sinθₜ     |     Brewster's angle: θ_B = arctan(n₂/n₁) \] Symbol Meaning n₁, n₂ refractive indices of the incident and transmitting media θᵢ, θₜ angles of incidence and refraction θ_B Brewster's angle, at which p-polarized reflectance is zero

docs.title: Interactive Controls
docs.text:
Launch the simulator and use the following controls on the Reflection, Refraction & Brewster's Angle for EM Waves panel:

Control Symbol Range Default Incident medium index n₁ 1–2 1 Transmitting medium index n₂ 1–2.5 1.5 Angle of incidence θᵢ 0–89 ° 30 ° Polarization (0=s, 1=p) pol 0–1 1

docs.title: Procedure
docs.text:
1. For p-polarized light, sweep the angle of incidence from 0° to 89° and plot the reflectance R_p(θᵢ); locate the angle where it drops to zero.
  2. Compare this angle with the calculated Brewster's angle θ_B = arctan(n₂/n₁) for the chosen n₁, n₂.
  3. Repeat for s-polarized light and confirm R_s never reaches zero at any angle (no Brewster angle exists for s-polarization).
  4. At θ = θ_B, verify numerically that the reflected and refracted rays are exactly perpendicular to each other.
  5. Vary n₂ (keeping n₁ fixed) and verify θ_B shifts accordingly; tabulate θ_B versus n₂/n₁.
  6. Send unpolarized light (an equal mix of s and p) at θ_B and confirm the reflected beam is purely s-polarized.

docs.title: Graph
docs.text:
Reflectance vs Angle of Incidence — R_p dips to zero at Brewster's angle; R_s does not

📋 Click the grid, then paste (Ctrl+V) a copied photo of your reading/meter trace to overlay it for tracing — nothing is uploaded, it stays on your screen only. Overlay opacity Clear pasted image Reflectance vs Angle of Incidence θᵢ (°) → Reflectance R → 15×15 grid — choose your own scale per square; plot from your observation table. Expected trend: R_p dips to zero at Brewster's angle; R_s does not

docs.title: Calculations
docs.text: Use the governing formula above with your tabulated observations to compute the required result for each row. Show at least one complete sample calculation with units.

docs.title: Result
docs.text: | n₂/n₁ | Brewster angle (measured) | Brewster angle (arctan n₂/n₁) | % deviation | |---|---|---|---|| | | | | |

docs.title: Precautions
docs.text:
• Ensure the angle of incidence is measured from the normal to the interface, not from the surface itself.
  • Distinguish carefully between s- and p-polarization when reading off reflectance curves — only p shows a Brewster null.
  • Keep n₁ < n₂ or n₁ > n₂ clearly in mind, since total internal reflection (a separate phenomenon) only occurs going from denser to rarer medium beyond the critical angle.
  • Use enough angular resolution near the expected Brewster angle to locate the null precisely.

docs.title: Maximum Permissible Error
docs.text:
\[ Δθ_B ≈ [1/(1+(n₂/n₁)²)]·(n₂/n₁)·(Δn₂/n₂ + Δn₁/n₁) \]

Obtained by differentiating θ_B = arctan(n₂/n₁) with respect to the ratio n₂/n₁.

docs.title: Viva-Voce Questions
docs.text:
1. Derive Brewster's angle condition from the Fresnel equation for p-polarized reflectance.
  2. Why does no Brewster angle exist for s-polarized light?
  3. Show that at Brewster's angle the reflected and refracted rays are perpendicular.
  4. How are Brewster windows used inside a laser cavity, and why?
  5. Distinguish Brewster's angle from the critical angle for total internal reflection.
  6. How do polarizing sunglasses exploit Brewster-angle reflection from horizontal surfaces like water or roads?

docs.title: Propagation in Dielectric & Conducting Media (Skin Depth)
docs.text: ← Back to BHP301 Course Page · Launch Simulator · 📝 Open Lab Record & Viva-Voce Booklet (PDF) (print just this experiment's pages when you sit down at the simulator)

docs.title: Propagation of EM Waves in Dielectric & Conducting Media (Skin Depth)
docs.text:
SOURCE / CONTROL APPARATUS (virtual setup) PROBE / DETECTOR A virtual EM wave incident on a slab of adjustable material (dielectric or conductor, with selectable εr, μr, σ), animated to show the wave slowing/attenuating inside, with a live plot of amplitude versus penetration depth.

Apparatus schematic · A virtual EM wave incident on a slab of adjustable material (dielectric or conductor, with selectable εr, μr, σ), animated to show the wave slowing/attenuating inside, with a live plot of amplitude versus penetration depth.

docs.title: Aim
docs.text: To study how the speed, wavelength, and attenuation of an electromagnetic wave change on entering a dielectric or a conducting medium, and to determine the skin depth for a conductor as a function of frequency and conductivity.

docs.title: Theoretical Background
docs.text: In a lossless dielectric of relative permittivity εr and relative permeability μr, an EM wave travels at reduced speed v = c/n where the refractive index n = √(μrεr), with no attenuation. In a conducting medium of conductivity σ, however, the wave is exponentially attenuated as it propagates because oscillating currents driven by E dissipate energy as heat (Ohmic loss). Its amplitude falls as e^{−z/δ}, where the skin depth δ = √(2/ωμσ) is the distance over which the amplitude drops to 1/e of its surface value. Skin depth decreases with increasing frequency and conductivity, which is why high-frequency currents in a good conductor are confined to a thin surface layer (the 'skin effect'), and why good conductors like metals are excellent reflectors and poor transmitters of high-frequency EM waves.

docs.title: Governing Formula
docs.text: \[ Dielectric: v = c/√(μrεr)     |     Conductor: δ = √(2/ωμσ) \] Symbol Meaning v wave speed inside the dielectric (m/s) n = √(μrεr) refractive index δ skin depth in a conductor (m) ω angular frequency (rad/s) σ conductivity (S/m)

docs.title: Interactive Controls
docs.text:
Launch the simulator and use the following controls on the Propagation of EM Waves in Dielectric & Conducting Media (Skin Depth) panel:

Control Symbol Range Default Relative permittivity (dielectric mode) εr 1–10 4 Wave frequency f 0.1–1000 MHz 100 MHz Conductivity (conductor mode) σ 0.1–60 MS/m 6 MS/m Medium (0=dielectric, 1=conductor) mode 0–1 0

docs.title: Procedure
docs.text:
1. In dielectric mode, vary εr and verify the wave speed inside falls as v = c/√εr (with μr = 1).
  2. Verify the wavelength inside the dielectric shrinks by the same factor √εr while the frequency stays fixed (set by the source).
  3. Switch to conductor mode and vary the frequency; plot δ vs f and verify δ ∝ 1/√f.
  4. At fixed frequency, vary the conductivity σ and verify δ ∝ 1/√σ.
  5. Compute the skin depth for copper (σ ≈ 6×10⁷ S/m) at 50 Hz and at 1 GHz, and comment on the enormous difference.
  6. Estimate, using the skin depth, roughly what thickness of a conducting enclosure is needed to shield against a given frequency (link to the shielding workshop).

docs.title: Observation Table
docs.text: | S. No. | Mode | Frequency f (MHz) | εr or σ | Wave speed / Skin depth | Wavelength (dielectric mode) | |---|---|---|---|---|---|| | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | |

docs.title: Graph
docs.text:
Skin Depth vs Frequency (log-log) — slope ≈ -1/2

📋 Click the grid, then paste (Ctrl+V) a copied photo of your reading/meter trace to overlay it for tracing — nothing is uploaded, it stays on your screen only. Overlay opacity Clear pasted image Skin Depth vs Frequency (log-log) log₁₀ f → log₁₀ δ → 15×15 grid — choose your own scale per square; plot from your observation table. Expected trend: slope ≈ -1/2

docs.title: Calculations
docs.text: Use the governing formula above with your tabulated observations to compute the required result for each row. Show at least one complete sample calculation with units.

docs.title: Result
docs.text: | Case | Measured quantity | Theoretical value | % deviation | |---|---|---|---|| | | | | |

docs.title: Precautions
docs.text:
• Keep μr = 1 (non-magnetic medium) unless specifically studying magnetic conductors, to isolate the effect of εr or σ alone.
  • Remember skin depth applies strictly to good conductors (σ/ωε ≫ 1); the formula is not valid for weakly-conducting or dielectric media.
  • Use consistent SI units (rad/s for ω, S/m for σ) when checking the numeric value of δ.
  • Note the refractive index model here assumes a non-dispersive dielectric; real materials' εr can vary with frequency.

docs.title: Maximum Permissible Error
docs.text:
\[ Δδ/δ = ½(Δf/f + Δσ/σ) \]

δ ∝ 1/√(fσ), so the fractional errors in f and σ are each halved before summing.

docs.title: Viva-Voce Questions
docs.text:
1. Derive the refractive index n = √(μrεr) from the wave equation in a dielectric medium.
  2. Why does an EM wave attenuate exponentially inside a conductor but not inside an ideal (lossless) dielectric?
  3. Derive the expression for skin depth starting from the wave equation with a conduction-current term.
  4. Why are radio antennas and high-frequency circuit traces often silver-plated even though bulk silver is only marginally more conductive than copper?
  5. How does skin depth explain why AC power transmission cables sometimes use stranded or hollow conductors?
  6. Relate the concept of skin depth to electromagnetic shielding effectiveness of an enclosure.

docs.title: WS3: Antenna Radiation Pattern & Gain
docs.text: ← Back to BHP301 Course Page · Launch Simulator

docs.title: WS3: Antenna Radiation Pattern & Gain Optimization
docs.text: 🔧 This is a hands-on industrial workshop — launch the simulator and open the WS3: Antenna Radiation Pattern & Gain Optimization panel to attempt it interactively. Your score is based on how close each control is set to its optimal (target) value.

docs.title: Scenario
docs.text: You are optimizing a dipole antenna array for a communications link. Adjust the element length, spacing, and phasing to maximize gain in the desired direction while keeping the sidelobe level and beamwidth within the specified mission requirements.

docs.title: Controls to Tune
docs.text: Control Unit Typical Range Notes Element length (in wavelengths) λ 0.3–1.0 optimal ≈ 0.5 λ Array element spacing λ 0.25–1.0 optimal ≈ 0.5 λ Inter-element phase shift ° 0–180 optimal ≈ 0 °

docs.title: What You'll Learn
docs.text:
• A half-wave dipole (length ≈ 0.5λ) is resonant and radiates efficiently with a well-known figure-8 pattern.
  • Combining elements into an array allows the radiation pattern to be shaped ('beamformed') by controlling element spacing and relative phase.
  • Element spacing near λ (with in-phase feeding) can introduce unwanted grating lobes, while too-close spacing increases mutual coupling losses.

docs.text:

  2. Start with each control at its mid-range value and observe the live readout/score.
  3. Adjust one control at a time, noting how the score changes, before combining adjustments.
  4. Iterate until your score is consistently above 90% — the simulator awards a printable completion certificate at this level.
  5. Once satisfied, note down the final values you converged on and compare them with the underlying formula's prediction of the true optimum.

docs.title: WS4: Eddy-Current Non-Destructive Testing
docs.text: ← Back to BHP301 Course Page · Launch Simulator

docs.title: WS4: Eddy-Current Non-Destructive Testing
docs.text: 🔧 This is a hands-on industrial workshop — launch the simulator and open the WS4: Eddy-Current Non-Destructive Testing panel to attempt it interactively. Your score is based on how close each control is set to its optimal (target) value.

docs.title: Scenario
docs.text: You are a quality-control inspector using eddy-current testing to find hidden cracks in an aluminium aircraft panel without damaging it. Tune the probe's coil frequency and lift-off distance to maximize flaw sensitivity while keeping the background signal noise low enough to avoid false alarms.

docs.title: Controls to Tune
docs.text: Control Unit Typical Range Notes Probe coil frequency kHz 1–500 optimal ≈ 100 kHz Probe lift-off distance mm 0.1–5 optimal ≈ 0.5 mm Signal amplifier gain dB 0–60 optimal ≈ 30 dB

docs.title: What You'll Learn
docs.text:
• An eddy-current probe induces circulating currents in a conductive sample; a crack disrupts these currents and changes the coil's impedance, which is detected as a signal.
  • Higher frequency confines eddy currents nearer the surface (thanks to the skin-depth effect) giving better sensitivity to surface cracks but less penetration depth for buried flaws.
  • Increasing the 'lift-off' (probe-to-surface gap) strongly reduces coupling and sensitivity — lift-off variation is one of the most common sources of false signals in the field.

  4. Iterate until your score is consistently above 90% — the simulator awards a printable completion certificate at this level.
  5. Once satisfied, note down the final values you converged on and compare them with the underlying formula's prediction of the true optimum.

docs.title: Safety Note
docs.text: Real eddy-current NDT equipment is low-power and safe, but proper calibration against reference standards is essential before any real inspection.

docs.text: ← Back to BHP301 Course Page · Launch Simulator

docs.title: WS1: EMI/EMC Compliance Testing
docs.text: 🔧 This is a hands-on industrial workshop — launch the simulator and open the WS1: EMI/EMC Compliance Testing panel to attempt it interactively. Your score is based on how close each control is set to its optimal (target) value.

docs.title: Scenario
docs.text: You are a compliance test engineer at a consumer-electronics company. A new switch-mode power supply must pass radiated-emission limits before it can be sold. Your job is to tune the product's shielding and filtering until the measured emission spectrum stays under the regulatory limit line across the required frequency band.

docs.title: Controls to Tune
docs.text: Control Unit Typical Range Notes Shielding effectiveness dB 0–80 optimal ≈ 45 dB EMI filter cutoff frequency kHz 10–500 optimal ≈ 150 kHz Ground-plane quality index % 0–100 optimal ≈ 85 %

docs.title: What You'll Learn
docs.text:
• Radiated emissions from switching circuits rise with dI/dt and loop area; shielding and grounding both reduce the effective radiating loop.
  • An EMI filter's cutoff must sit below the switching frequency's harmonics that are closest to the regulatory limit, without needlessly attenuating the wanted signal.
  • Compliance test labs use a calibrated antenna in an anechoic or open-area test site and compare the measured spectrum against a regulatory limit line (e.g. CISPR/FCC Class B).

docs.title: WS6: HV Insulation & Breakdown Testing
docs.text: ← Back to BHP301 Course Page · Launch Simulator

docs.title: WS6: High-Voltage Insulation & Breakdown (Paschen) Testing
docs.text: 🔧 This is a hands-on industrial workshop — launch the simulator and open the WS6: High-Voltage Insulation & Breakdown (Paschen) Testing panel to attempt it interactively. Your score is based on how close each control is set to its optimal (target) value.

docs.title: Scenario
docs.text: You are testing the insulation of a high-voltage gas-filled switchgear enclosure. Adjust the gas pressure and electrode gap to keep the system operating safely below its breakdown voltage, using the Paschen curve to avoid the dangerous low-pressure breakdown minimum.

docs.title: Controls to Tune
docs.text: Control Unit Typical Range Notes Gas pressure kPa 1–300 optimal ≈ 100 kPa Electrode gap mm 0.5–20 optimal ≈ 8 mm Applied test voltage kV 1–50 optimal ≈ 20 kV

docs.title: What You'll Learn
docs.text:
• Paschen's law shows breakdown voltage depends on the product of pressure and gap (pd), not on either alone, and has a characteristic minimum at a specific pd value.
  • Operating near the Paschen minimum is dangerous because a small drop in pressure (e.g. from a leak) can sharply reduce the breakdown voltage rather than increase it, counter to naive intuition.
  • Increasing the gap at fixed pressure eventually raises breakdown voltage again (right side of the Paschen curve), which is why high-voltage equipment is designed with generous clearances.

  5. Once satisfied, note down the final values you converged on and compare them with the underlying formula's prediction of the true optimum.

docs.title: WS5: Magnetic Shielding Enclosure Design
docs.text: ← Back to BHP301 Course Page · Launch Simulator

docs.title: WS5: Magnetic Shielding Enclosure Design
docs.text: 🔧 This is a hands-on industrial workshop — launch the simulator and open the WS5: Magnetic Shielding Enclosure Design panel to attempt it interactively. Your score is based on how close each control is set to its optimal (target) value.

docs.title: Scenario
docs.text: You must design a mu-metal enclosure to protect a sensitive magnetometer from stray laboratory fields. Choose the shield material, thickness, and number of nested layers to achieve the required shielding factor without making the enclosure impractically large or expensive.

docs.title: Controls to Tune
docs.text: Control Unit Typical Range Notes Shield material relative permeability ×10³ 1–100 optimal ≈ 50 ×10³ Shield wall thickness mm 0.2–5 optimal ≈ 1.5 mm Number of nested shield layers 1–4 optimal ≈ 2

docs.title: What You'll Learn
docs.text:
• High-permeability materials (mu-metal) shield low-frequency and DC magnetic fields by providing a low-reluctance path that diverts flux around the protected volume, unlike electric-field shielding which relies on free charge.
  • Shielding factor improves with wall thickness and permeability, but with strongly diminishing returns — doubling thickness rarely doubles the shielding factor.
  • Nesting multiple shields with air gaps between them multiplies the overall shielding factor and is often more effective than a single thick shield of the same total material.

docs.title: WS7: RF Impedance Matching & VSWR
docs.text: ← Back to BHP301 Course Page · Launch Simulator

docs.title: WS7: RF Impedance Matching & VSWR Minimization
docs.text: 🔧 This is a hands-on industrial workshop — launch the simulator and open the WS7: RF Impedance Matching & VSWR Minimization panel to attempt it interactively. Your score is based on how close each control is set to its optimal (target) value.

docs.title: Scenario
docs.text: You are commissioning an RF transmitter feeding an antenna through a coaxial cable. Tune a matching network (adjustable reactive elements) so that the load impedance seen by the transmitter is as close as possible to the cable's characteristic impedance, minimizing the voltage standing-wave ratio (VSWR) and reflected power.

docs.title: Controls to Tune
docs.text: Control Unit Typical Range Notes Shunt matching reactance Ω -200–200 optimal ≈ 0 Ω Series matching reactance Ω -200–200 optimal ≈ 0 Ω Load resistance seen at antenna feed Ω 10–200 optimal ≈ 50 Ω

docs.title: What You'll Learn
docs.text:
• VSWR = (1+|Γ|)/(1−|Γ|), where Γ is the reflection coefficient at the load; a perfectly matched load (Z_load = Z₀) gives Γ = 0 and VSWR = 1.
  • An L-section matching network of series and shunt reactive elements can transform almost any load impedance to match a transmission line's characteristic impedance at a single frequency.
  • High VSWR reflects power back toward the transmitter, reducing delivered power and potentially damaging the transmitter's output stage if severe enough.

docs.title: WS2: Transformer Design & Testing
docs.text: ← Back to BHP301 Course Page · Launch Simulator

docs.title: WS2: Transformer Design & Testing
docs.text: 🔧 This is a hands-on industrial workshop — launch the simulator and open the WS2: Transformer Design & Testing panel to attempt it interactively. Your score is based on how close each control is set to its optimal (target) value.

docs.title: Scenario
docs.text: You are asked to design a step-down transformer for a specified turns ratio and to minimize losses. Adjust the primary/secondary turns, core material, and winding parameters until the transformer delivers the required output voltage with maximum efficiency and acceptably low core loss.

docs.title: Controls to Tune
docs.text: Control Unit Typical Range Notes Turns ratio N₁:N₂ (target set by task) 2–20 optimal ≈ 10 Core relative permeability ×10³ 1–10 optimal ≈ 5 ×10³ Winding resistance (per winding) mΩ 10–500 optimal ≈ 80 mΩ

docs.title: What You'll Learn
docs.text:
• An ideal transformer's voltage ratio equals its turns ratio, V₂/V₁ = N₂/N₁, and its power is conserved (V₁I₁ = V₂I₂).
  • Real transformers lose energy to winding resistance (copper loss, ∝I²R) and to core hysteresis and eddy-current losses (core loss, roughly independent of load).
  • A higher core permeability reduces the magnetizing current needed and improves efficiency, but real cores saturate above a material-dependent flux density.
